posts.summary: Chris Riccomini announces the launch of Materialized View, a newsletter focused on software infrastructure. The newsletter originated from a Twitter thread sharing interesting infrastructure projects that gained unexpected traction. Rather than continuing to post scattered content across multiple platforms, Riccomini decided to consolidate his thoughts into a regular newsletter. The publication will cover infrastructure news, developer interviews, stack deep dives, and project highlights on a biweekly schedule.
key_points: Materialized View will cover software infrastructure news, developer interviews, stack deep dives, and project/paper highlights
The newsletter originated organically from a popular Twitter thread about infrastructure projects
Content will be published approximately every two weeks with 3-4 items per issue
The newsletter is free but premium content may be added based on reader interest
money_quotes: Rather than posting things scattershot on Twitter, Bluesky, Github, and in private DMs and texts, I'm going to share my thoughts publicly and regularly in one place: Materialized View.
The newsletter will go out like messages in a Kafka producer with a linger.ms of 2 weeks and a batch.size of 3-4 items.
posts.key_insight: There's clear demand for curated, thoughtful coverage of software infrastructure that goes beyond scattered social media posts.

posts.summary: This newsletter roundup covers several developments in database infrastructure, with a focus on SQLite's growing role in distributed systems. Bluesky is moving their Personal Data Server from PostgreSQL to a distributed SQLite implementation, signaling SQLite's increasing adoption. Nile launched a serverless Postgres-compatible database purpose-built for SaaS multi-tenancy. The author also explores buffer pool implementations after diving into research on mmap in databases, and highlights Tigris, an open-source serverless NoSQL database built on FoundationDB.
key_points: Bluesky is migrating from monolithic PostgreSQL to distributed SQLite for their PDS servers, reflecting a broader trend of SQLite in distributed systems
Litestream and LiteFS enable SQLite replication by intercepting transactions through a virtual filesystem proxy
Nile's serverless Postgres database moves multi-tenancy concerns from application code to the database layer, eliminating the need for libraries like Rails's apartment
Buffer pools use LRU eviction strategies, with implementations like MySQL's InnoDB inserting pages into the middle of the pool in specific ways
Tigris is an open-source serverless NoSQL database based on Uber's Docstore, built on FoundationDB with strict serializability
money_quotes: SQLite is showing up more and more in distributed systems. I think this trend will continue.

LiteFS works by interposing a very thin virtual filesystem between your app and your on-disk database file. It's not a file system like ext4, but rather a pass-through. Think of it as a file system proxy.
This works particularly well for SaaS applications where each customer is isolated from one another.
Most people who've worked on SaaS immediately get why this database is a big deal.
With Nile, all of this moves to the database layer. Pretty slick.
Docstore is a general-purpose multi-model database that provides a strict serializability consistency model on a partition level and can scale horizontally to serve high volume workloads.
posts.key_insight: SQLite is increasingly being adopted in distributed systems architectures, while purpose-built databases like Nile are pushing multi-tenancy concerns from application code down to the database layer.

posts.summary: This newsletter issue covers four main topics: HTTP/3 and its underlying QUIC protocol, Responsive's new autoscaler for Kafka Streams, the WiscKey paper on SSD-optimized LSM storage, and LittleHorse as a durable execution framework. The author explains how QUIC reimagines TCP with built-in TLS, multiplexing, connection migration, and extensibility. He reflects on his own experience building Apache Samza and the problems with static resource allocation that Responsive's autoscaler addresses. The WiscKey discussion highlights how separating keys from values in LSM trees reduces write amplification for SSDs, an architecture now widely adopted.
key_points: HTTP/3's improvements over HTTP/2 come almost entirely from QUIC, which is essentially a reimagined TCP running over UDP

Responsive's Kafka Streams autoscaler addresses the fundamental problem of static resource allocation by letting developers define scaling rules based on latency and throughput rather than physical resources
WiscKey's key insight is that SSD characteristics (cheap random reads, parallelism, write wear) justify separating keys and values in LSM trees to reduce write amplification
LittleHorse represents a unique blend of batch workflow systems, durable execution frameworks, and traditional business process automation
The author draws from personal experience building Apache Samza to contextualize why dynamic resource allocation matters
money_quotes: We've reimagined and implemented a much more advanced version of TCP and called it QUIC. And because we want to make QUIC easier to deploy, we run it over UDP.
Two things always annoyed me about the way I implemented job sizing in Samza: Jobs were defined by physical requirements (memory, cpu, disk, network). Resource requirements were static.
Both are undesirable because it's hard for application developers to guess physical requirements and because resource requirements change over time.
Since 2016, WiscKey's architecture has been widely adopted for LSM stores.
I think of it as a mashup between three different framework styles: Airflow/Prefect/Dagster-style workflows, Durable execution frameworks, Traditional business process modeling and automation.
posts.key_insight: Modern infrastructure increasingly benefits from separating concerns—whether it's compute from state in stream processing, keys from values in storage engines, or protocol layers in networking—to optimize for new hardware and deployment realities.

posts.summary: This newsletter edition surveys the resurgence of Viewstamped Replication, a consistency algorithm predating Paxos and Raft, highlighting a new Go implementation alongside existing Zig and Rust versions. Riccomini connects this to the broader theme of deterministic simulation testing, examining approaches from Dropbox, TigerBeetle, and FoundationDB. Dropbox's sync engine rewrite demonstrates how single-threaded control flow enables serialized, reproducible tests. TigerBeetle advocates reducing variability in both IO and time to improve debugging, testing, and runtime performance. FoundationDB's Simulation framework introduces techniques like 'swizzle-clogging' to find deep edge cases. The core takeaway is that stamping out non-determinism and using seeded pseudo-random testing enables reproducible failures.
key_points: Viewstamped Replication is experiencing renewed interest as an alternative to Paxos and Raft, with new implementations appearing in Go, Rust, and Zig
Dropbox achieved deterministic testing by running nearly all code on a single control thread and serializing async operations
TigerBeetle recommends reducing variability in both space (IO) and time (clocks) for easier debugging and better performance
FoundationDB's 'swizzle-clogging' technique randomly clogging and unclogging network connections finds rare edge-case bugs
The pattern across all systems: eliminate non-determinism and use seeded pseudo-random numbers for reproducible test failures
money_quotes: Viewstamped Replication is a consistency algorithm that predates Paxos and Raft.
Say goodbye to flaky tests! This single-threaded design is the key to the determinism and reproducibility of our randomized testing systems.
Alex recommends reducing variability in both space (IO) and time (clocks).
To swizzle-clog, you first pick a random subset of nodes in the cluster. Then, you 'clog' (stop) each of their network connections one by one over a few seconds.
This pattern seems to be particularly good at finding deep issues that only happen in the rarest real-world cases.
This is harder than it sounds, hence all of this wonderful content.
posts.key_insight: Deterministic simulation testing—achieved by eliminating non-determinism and using seeded pseudo-random execution—is the gold standard for testing distributed systems, as demonstrated by Dropbox, TigerBeetle, and FoundationDB.

posts.summary: The durable execution space has become overcrowded with startups following Temporal's success, but the market cannot sustain them all without significant expansion. The author argues that frameworks remain too difficult to use, limiting adoption beyond canonical use cases like payment processing. Stream processing represents the biggest opportunity for convergence, as both paradigms face identical challenges around non-determinism, idempotency, and schema evolution. Second-generation frameworks that unify stream processing and durable execution with friendlier APIs could justify the current bubble by expanding the total addressable market.
key_points: Durable execution is essentially workflow orchestration with transactional state management, persisting each step so code can resume after failures
The market has too many startups (Temporal, Restate, Orkes, LittleHorse, etc.) and can't sustain them without expanding beyond payment/shopping cart use cases
Adoption costs are too high because frameworks are too hard to use—even Temporal's Python course is daunting
Stream processing and durable execution face the same hard problems: non-determinism, idempotency, schema evolution, dead letter queues
Log-based architectures (like Kafka) are becoming standard for durable execution, making convergence with stream processing a logical next step
money_quotes: The current market can't sustain this many startups. For even a few big winners, the market has to become much larger.
The adoption cost for durable execution is too high because the frameworks are too hard to use.
I don't use these frameworks unless I have to.
Many of the common pitfalls that Chris lists in his post are exactly the same problems that make stream processing hard.
Right now, we have a collection of first-generation durable execution and stream processing frameworks. No one has really nailed the hard parts and the APIs are clunky.
That's not a dig on any of these teams; these problems are really, really tough. I have the scars to prove it.
Where a16z sees a database, I see a write-ahead log.
posts.key_insight: The durable execution bubble can only be justified if second-generation frameworks converge with stream processing to create more user-friendly APIs that expand the market beyond payment processing.

posts.summary: This roundup post covers four data infrastructure topics: the emergence of transactional key-value stores built on S3 for serverless OLTP databases, the surprisingly diverse production use cases for Apache Flink beyond analytics, a balanced rebuttal defending mmap for database storage, and Titan as a new Terraform-like tool for data warehouse configuration. Riccomini argues the industry needs more open source transactional KV stores on S3 to serve as building blocks for serverless databases. He notes his expectation that Flink would be mostly SQL and CDC workloads was wrong—it's used for real-time ads, bot detection, and safety systems. The post also highlights the tension between pragmatic and academic approaches to database design through the mmap debate.
key_points: OLTP databases like Neon and TiDB are successfully using S3 as primary storage through various approaches: KV-based APIs with consensus protocols, WAL-based APIs, read-through caches, and write-back caches

Flink's production usage is more diverse than expected—extending well beyond analytics engineering and SQL to include real-time ads, bot detection, pricing, and safety applications at companies like Uber, Yelp, and Reddit
The mmap debate reveals a tension between pragmatic engineering (letting the OS handle caching, concurrency, eviction) and academic critique—both perspectives have merit

Titan bridges a gap in the data ecosystem by providing Terraform-like declarative configuration for data warehouse objects, which analytics engineers find more accessible than traditional infrastructure-as-code tools
Durable execution frameworks are proliferating rapidly, with over a dozen options now available for building fault-tolerant workflows
money_quotes: We need more open source transactional key-value stores on S3 with a variety of consensus protocols, APIs, and features.
Serverless databases could use these stores as a building block.
I expected Flink usage to mostly be analytics engineering use cases and SQL. My hypothesis was born of the deluge of Flink SQL and change data capture (CDC) content lately.
To my surprise, Flink's usage is quite diverse!
It's great to see such a well written and fair response. It's also nice to contrast pragmatic and academic perspectives.
For more complex use cases (especially in the data warehouse), data engineers are forced into dbt, Python scripts, templating languages, and custom tools.
Other data warehouse users—analytics engineers—often lack Terraform experience.
posts.key_insight: The data infrastructure ecosystem is evolving toward S3-based transactional storage and declarative configuration tools, while production streaming workloads are far more diverse than the industry's content focus on SQL and CDC would suggest.

posts.summary: This newsletter roundup covers four infrastructure topics: AWS Time Sync Service's new microsecond-level accuracy via hardware clocks on the Nitro platform, an introduction to SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) and its applications from vector search to LLMs, and the Epoxy paper which presents a protocol for distributed transactions across heterogeneous data stores without requiring XA protocol support. The author connects AWS's precision timing improvements to Google Spanner's TrueTime concept, while noting SIMD's growing relevance for AI workloads. Epoxy offers both isolation and atomicity for cross-database transactions through a shim-based architecture.
key_points: AWS Time Sync Service now provides double-digit microsecond accuracy via PTP Hardware Clocks on the Nitro platform, enabling better distributed system coordination similar to Google's TrueTime
SIMD enables CPUs to perform operations on multiple data points simultaneously, which is increasingly important for vector search, LLM inference, and common array operations
Epoxy protocol improves on X/Open XA by providing transactional isolation (not just atomicity) across heterogeneous data stores without requiring the stores themselves to implement any protocol
AWS Nitro provides modular building blocks for assembling hardware profiles with strong security and isolation guarantees
money_quotes: Reasoning about time globally is really helpful for distributed systems.
Time Sync now runs with double-digit microsecond accuracy.
Matrix operations are useful—something I wish I knew when taking Linear Algebra in college—particularly for vector search and running LLMs on CPUs.
Nearly every modern video game console since 1998 has incorporated a SIMD processor somewhere in its architecture.
Epoxy is a protocol for providing transactions across heterogeneous data stores.
Unlike X/Open XA, Epoxy provides transactional isolation, not just atomicity.
Epoxy isn't without tradeoffs, though.
posts.key_insight: Precision timing, SIMD parallelism, and cross-database transaction protocols represent foundational infrastructure capabilities that enable increasingly sophisticated distributed systems and AI workloads.

posts.summary: This newsletter edition covers three major developments in data infrastructure: the growing adoption of S3 as a storage layer for streaming systems, an architectural analysis of Neon DB's innovative PostgreSQL design, and an introduction to SKDB as a novel reactive edge database. The post examines different architectural approaches to S3-based streaming including WarpStream's fully stateless model, tiered storage implementations like AutoMQ and RedPanda, and streaming data lakes like Paimon. Riccomini notes that Kafka finally added tiered storage in version 3.6.0 after Confluent kept it as a proprietary feature. The piece highlights Neon's elegant design that replaces PostgreSQL's storage layer with remote storage interfaces and praises SKDB's ground-up approach to building a concurrent, reactive database with materialized views and subscriptions.
key_points: S3 is increasingly being adopted as a viable storage layer for streaming, with three distinct architectural approaches: S3 as source of truth (WarpStream), tiered storage (AutoMQ, RedPanda), and streaming data lakes (Paimon)
Neon DB elegantly separates PostgreSQL's storage into a remote WAL and page service atop S3, using Paxos over RAFT and QEMU over Firecracker for live migration support
Kafka finally released tiered storage in 3.6.0 as early-access, after Confluent had kept this capability as a paid proprietary feature
SKDB is a proof-of-concept reactive edge database rebuilt from scratch to support materialized views, subscriptions, and high concurrency—distinct from SQLite forks
money_quotes: More and more people are buying into the idea that S3 is a good storage layer for streaming.
Neon is such an elegant project.
I had assumed this was already available, but it turns out Confluent was keeping it as a paid feature.
Neon takes PostgreSQL and replaces its storage layer with remote storage interfaces.
Unlike SQLite and libsql, SKDB is rebuilt from the ground up to support such use cases.
SKDB is a new kind of database: a reactive edge database that supports materialized views, table subscriptions, and diff'ing.
posts.key_insight: The convergence of object storage like S3 with streaming and database systems is creating new architectural patterns that separate storage from compute in increasingly elegant ways.

posts.summary: This newsletter roundup covers three main infrastructure topics: AWS's S3 Express One Zone, zero-downtime VM live migrations, and Google's HighwayHash. Riccomini aggregates community opinions on S3 Express, expressing skepticism about its pricing despite acknowledging it as a useful building block. He highlights Felicitas Pojtinger's standing-ovation Kubecon talk on stateful VM migrations using network block device protocol, which enables microVMs like Firecracker to support live migrations. The post also spotlights HighwayHash, a SIMD-optimized hashing library that achieves 5.2x speedup over SipHash.
key_points: S3 Express One Zone offers low latency (<10ms) in a single AZ, but the community consensus is the pricing undermines its value proposition
Despite S3 Express's capabilities, we still need caches and write-ahead logs in front of S3 for cost reduction and high availability
Zero-downtime live migration of stateful VMs is now possible for microVMs like Firecracker through Loophole Labs' r3map and architekt tools
ML/AI process migration is increasingly important as GPUs and compute become scarce, with companies like Microsoft and Facebook already having this capability
HighwayHash achieves 5.2x speedup over SipHash using SIMD multiply and permute instructions, though it lacks formal cryptographic proofs
money_quotes: Personally, I'm speedrunning the hype cycle wave and living in the trough of disillusionment right now.
Express is a helpful building block, but it doesn't change storage architectures much. We still need caches and write-ahead logs (WALs) in front of S3 to reduce cost and keep high availability.
I've been cheerleading S3-as-primary-storage for a while now. It's great to see AWS show some love to this use case.
With r3map, microVMs like Firecracker and gVisor can now support live migrations just like heavier-weight VMs.
ML/AI process migration is of particular interest to me. Microsoft and Facebook have had this technology for a while. GPUs and compute are hard to come by.
We are not experienced cryptographers, and do not see a way to reduce the HighwayHash algorithm to provably secure constructions.
posts.key_insight: S3 Express represents AWS's acknowledgment of S3-as-primary-storage use cases, but its pricing means fundamental storage architectures requiring caches and WALs won't change anytime soon.

posts.summary: Riccomini explores why there's no true cloud-hosted Prometheus, discovering that its single-node storage design is intentional rather than a limitation. This architecture prioritizes simplicity for getting started while offering flexibility through remote storage integrations when scaling becomes necessary. He then pivots to searching for an embedded time-series database purpose-built for metrics workloads, ultimately discovering FrostDB from PolarSignals. FrostDB uses an LSM tree structure with Apache Arrow and Parquet, designed specifically for high-write, immutable observability data with dynamic schemas.
key_points: Prometheus deliberately uses single-node storage to simplify initial setup, deferring the complexity of distributed systems until users actually need to scale
The lack of true cloud-hosted Prometheus is by design—users can choose from dozens of remote storage backends when scaling out
Metrics workloads have distinct characteristics (high writes, low reads, immutable data, wide/dynamic schemas) that general OLAP databases don't optimize for
FrostDB is an embeddable columnar database using an LSM tree structure with Arrow for hot data and Parquet for cold storage
Parca leverages FrostDB to provide continuous CPU profiling through a single eBPF filter
money_quotes: I was surprised to find that there isn't any Prometheus cloud hosting.
Prometheus is intentionally designed with only single-node storage.
Getting started is easy because you don't have to set up a distributed system. Users can defer the scaling decision until later.
This architecture is all about user choice. Kudos to the Prometheus designers.
OLAP DBs should work, but I was really looking for something purpose-built for common metric characteristics.
It's built exactly as I'd hoped—a log-structured merge-tree (LSM). The first level stores Apache Arrow records. Data is then rewritten into Parquet for subsequent levels.
posts.key_insight: Prometheus's single-node architecture is a deliberate design choice that prioritizes simplicity and user choice over built-in distributed storage, enabling an ecosystem of remote backends rather than locking users into one scaling path.

posts.summary: This year-end post serves as a curated reading list, sharing the browser tabs Chris accumulated but couldn't get to throughout 2023. Rather than offering predictions or retrospectives, it catalogs papers and articles across major data infrastructure themes that captured his attention. The post highlights emerging trends including durable execution frameworks, S3-based persistence for streaming systems, HTAP databases, embedded databases at the edge, and PostgreSQL's continued dominance. Chris reflects on how the convergence toward object storage might reshape database architectures, particularly for hybrid workloads. The post also marks the two-month anniversary of Materialized View, with a nod to his most popular piece on durable execution.
key_points: Durable execution frameworks saw explosive growth in 2023, with Temporal leading but many new challengers emerging
The trend toward S3 persistence in streaming (exemplified by WarpStream) is reshaping how streaming systems are architected
The convergence of data on S3 raises interesting questions about whether HTAP and multi-model databases become easier to build
Embedded and edge databases (Litestream, LiteFS, libsql, Turso, SKDB) represent an active area of innovation
PostgreSQL and its extension ecosystem remain a pragmatic solution for an increasingly wide range of use cases
money_quotes: I've been thinking lately about what it means to have all our data directly on S3.
Does it make hybrid transaction/analytical processing (HTAP) and multi-model databases easier to build or more likely to be successful?
PostgreSQL and its extensions continue to be a pragmatic solution to, well, everything.
Analytics Twitter continues to be very—obnoxiously—loud.
The trend toward S3 persistence for streaming captured my interest.
posts.key_insight: The growing convergence of data systems on S3-based object storage is opening new architectural possibilities for HTAP, streaming, and embedded databases that may reshape the database landscape.

posts.summary: The post examines how ANSI SQL '92 database isolation levels are fundamentally broken, synthesizing recent research from Jepsen, Gwen Shapira, and others. The author argues that the 1992 standard left room for unspecified anomalies, and different databases implement isolation levels inconsistently. While some argue developers haven't noticed these issues, the author counters that these bugs are simply hard to detect and reproduce. Drawing from personal experience in payments processing, he emphasizes that database anomalies cause real harm through hours of debugging and unhappy customers. The takeaway is that developers should understand their database's actual behavior rather than trusting isolation level names.
key_points: ANSI SQL '92 isolation levels have fundamental gaps that allow unspecified anomalies, even at the Serializable level
The same isolation level can mean different things in different databases, making 'Repeatable Read' an unreliable guarantee
Jepsen's MySQL analysis found violations of internal consistency and Monotonic Atomic View that go beyond spec ambiguity
Database anomalies are hard to detect because they occur rarely and are difficult to reproduce, leading developers to blame other causes
Standards bodies need to define more rigorous isolation specifications to address these long-standing issues
money_quotes: Different databases support different isolation levels, and the same isolation level can mean different things in different databases.
It took one of the greatest software minds of a generation to prove that one of the most popular databases of all time was broken.
Developers that are bitten by database anomalies often don't know.
Applications exhibit some weird anomaly, which developers can't reproduce. Engineers throw up their hands, shrug, and move on. They never even know the database was the culprit.
Jepsen is doing us a favor by showing us that the almighty OLTP database is in fact fallible.
Make sure you understand how your database behaves (as best you can) and act accordingly. Caveat emptor.
posts.key_insight: Database isolation levels are ambiguously defined and inconsistently implemented, causing subtle bugs that developers struggle to diagnose because they never suspect the database itself.

posts.summary: Gunnar Morling launched the '1 billion row challenge' (1brc), tasking developers with using Java to parse 1 billion rows of temperature data and calculate statistics for each city. The challenge has gone viral, with developers experimenting with modern Java features like SIMD, virtual threads, and memory mapping to optimize performance. The current leader achieved 14 seconds, with hardware variations causing significant performance differences. Riccomini notes that the challenge is essentially reimplementing parts of Apache Arrow, and that NVMe speeds mean the bottleneck is text parsing rather than I/O.
key_points: The 1brc challenge is essentially reimplementing part of Apache Arrow in Java, as Frederic Branczyk pointed out
Modern Java features being tested include SIMD, virtual threads, ZGC garbage collection, and GraalVM
NVMe drives are so fast that reading 12GB takes only seconds—text parsing is the real bottleneck
Hardware variability is significant: the same implementation runs in 2 seconds on M3 but 12 seconds on M2
Optimization strategies include parallel processing, memory mapping, custom hashtables, and SIMD-based text parsing
money_quotes: Gunnar Morling nerd snipes the internet into building Arrow in Java.
NVMe's are insanely fast—it takes only a couple of seconds to read the 12 gigs of data on my M2.
Most of the focus has been on speeding up the text parsing.
My own implementation clocks in at a measly 53 seconds. I never got around to adding SIMD.
If you attempt this project, beware that hardware is quite variable.
posts.key_insight: A viral coding challenge reveals that modern Java has powerful optimization features most developers don't use, and that with NVMe storage, text parsing—not I/O—is now the performance bottleneck.

posts.summary: Chris Riccomini interviews Frederic Branczyk, founder of Polar Signals, about their always-on, zero-instrumentation profiler. The discussion covers Polar Signals's architecture, particularly Parca and FrostDB. Parca achieves minimal invasiveness by using an eBPF filter that samples the entire OS stack at 19hz. The collected data is stored in FrostDB, an embedded storage engine built on Datafusion and Parquet. The interview also references several influential papers on profiling and optimization from Google.
key_points: Polar Signals offers always-on, zero-instrumentation profiling through Parca
Parca uses eBPF filters to sample the entire OS stack at 19hz for minimal invasiveness
FrostDB is an embedded storage engine built on Datafusion and Parquet
The architecture draws inspiration from Google's continuous profiling research
money_quotes: Parca is able to achieve its minimally invasive profiling claims by using an eBPF filter that samples the entire OS's stack at 19hz
FrostDB, an embedded storage engine built on Datafusion and Parquet
posts.key_insight: Modern continuous profiling can be achieved with minimal overhead by combining eBPF sampling with columnar storage engines built on Datafusion and Parquet.

posts.summary: This newsletter edition covers three main topics: Apple's L4S protocol for reducing network latency in realtime applications, terminal-based benchmarking tools surfaced by the 1brc challenge, and Zed's unified data model compared to Recap. L4S works by having clients signal congestion notification support and servers report queued packets, allowing clients to throttle their send rate. The benchmarking section highlights hyperfine, poop, and flameshow as useful performance analysis tools. Riccomini expresses validation that Zed's data model independently arrived at similar design choices as his Recap project, while noting Zed is more ambitious with its full ecosystem including serialization formats and query languages.
key_points: L4S reduces network queuing and packet loss by having clients and servers communicate congestion state through explicit notification bits, benefiting realtime video and gaming

The 1brc challenge has surfaced useful terminal benchmarking tools including hyperfine (cross-platform with statistical analysis), poop (Linux-only with hardware counters), and flameshow (terminal flamegraph viewer)
Zed's data model unifies JSON and relational models similarly to Recap, validating independent design choices made by both projects
Zed offers a complete ecosystem (serialization, query language, table formats) while Recap focuses narrowly on schema reading, writing, and conversion with slightly more robust type support
money_quotes: L4S is based on the insight that the root cause of queuing delay is in the capacity-seeking congestion controllers of senders, not in the queue itself.
Realtime applications—video and gaming—are an important part of our lives. These applications don't do well when their network path is congested.
Since L4S sits low in the network stack—in both layer 3 (IP) and layer 4 (TCP/UDP) of the OSI model—your code should automatically benefit without any changes.
Zed is a new data model that unifies the JSON and relational models to make data as easy as ever.
I am happy to see that Zed's data model is very similar to Recap's. We both made the same design choices independently. This is validating and bodes well for both projects.
Recap is much slimmer; it deliberately limits itself to reading, writing, and converting between service, event, and database schemas.
posts.key_insight: Independent convergence on similar data model designs between Zed and Recap suggests these unified schema approaches are solving real problems in the right way.

posts.summary: This is a brief podcast episode announcement introducing an interview with Julien Verlaguet, founder of SkipLabs. The post previews a conversation about SKDB, a reactive database featuring performant materialized views, database diffing, and streaming via ephemeral tables. Chris mentions that SkipLabs has launched and directs readers to their websites for more information. The post is primarily promotional rather than substantive analysis.
key_points: SKDB is a reactive database with performant materialized views, diffing between databases, and streaming via ephemeral tables
SkipStorage and Skiplang serve as building blocks for SKDB
Julien Verlaguet previously spent 9 years at Facebook as tech lead for Hack and Skiplang
money_quotes: SKDB is built as a reactive database that features performant materialized views, diffing between databases, and streaming via ephemeral tables
posts.key_insight: SKDB represents a new approach to reactive applications by combining materialized views, database diffing, and streaming capabilities into a unified database platform.

posts.summary: Chris Riccomini responds to Gunnar Morling's post about change data capture (CDC) and data contracts, arguing that while data contracts are indeed the right solution to CDC's encapsulation problems, the proposed implementations (outbox pattern and stream processing) don't actually deliver what's needed. He contends that developers need integrated tools that handle schema versioning, compatibility detection, and SLA management—not a patchwork of separate tools. Riccomini emphasizes that the hardest part of data contracts isn't technical but cultural, involving complex cross-team coordination around data ownership and priorities. He concludes by advocating for purpose-built products like Gable that address these holistic challenges rather than just transformation mechanics.
key_points: CDC exposes internal database schemas to downstream consumers, creating coupling problems including schema breakage, accidental PII leakage, and loss of transaction semantics
Outbox patterns and stream processors can transform data but don't implement true data contracts with compatibility rules, SLAs, and governance
Asking developers to cobble together Protobuf, Flink, schema registries, and governance tools feels like 'your data team is giving you the middle finger'
The hardest part of data contracts is organizational culture—managing cross-team coordination around data ownership, priorities, and breaking changes
Purpose-built products that facilitate cross-team collaboration are needed, not just transformation tools
money_quotes: To an application developer, this feels like your data team is giving you the middle finger.
Engineers discovering their data is consumed downstream is but one gentle example of cross-team coordination complexity.
Throwing more tools at the problem without addressing culture is not going to help.
Application developers want an integrated solution that makes it easy to safely evolve schemas and maintain SLAs.
But the hardest part of data contracts aren't even the tools, it's managing the data culture at organizations.
Neither stream processors nor the outbox pattern do this.
posts.key_insight: Data contracts solve CDC's encapsulation problems in theory, but implementing them requires integrated tooling and cultural change around cross-team data ownership—not just stream processors and outbox tables.

posts.summary: The database industry is undergoing a fundamental shift toward disassembly, where monolithic databases are being broken into reusable components like query parsers, optimizers, and storage engines. This trend traces back to Hadoop's separation of compute, data, and control planes eighteen years ago, and continues today with projects like Apache Arrow's DataFusion providing complete database toolkits. PostgreSQL's robust extension API and storage layer have enabled similar disassembly in the RDBMS space, with projects like Neon making even the write-ahead log pluggable. The author predicts this will lead to commoditized data warehouses, unified OLTP/OLAP systems, and poses a serious threat to Kafka's data integration use case as systems converge on object stores.
key_points: Hadoop pioneered database disassembly by separating compute (MapReduce), data (HDFS), and control planes (YARN) - an architecture that persists today with Spark, S3, and Kubernetes
Apache Arrow's DataFusion is emerging as a complete database toolkit that can be used as an integrated query engine or as composable libraries for individual layers
PostgreSQL's extension API and storage layer make it ideal for disassembly, with Neon taking this further by making the WAL pluggable with Paxos-based remote storage
Database performance and features will become commoditized as shared components make it easier to add, copy, or share capabilities across systems
Kafka faces an existential threat as OLTP and OLAP systems converge on object stores, eliminating the need for separate data integration infrastructure
money_quotes: Eighteen years ago, Hadoop broke the data warehouse into compute, data, and control planes—a paradigm that persists to this day.
A specific database might spend the majority of its innovation tokens in just one layer; say, the optimizer.

Unlike the projects above, which focus on a single layer, DataFusion does everything. You can use it as an integrated query engine like Trino or DuckDB, or you can use it as a library for any one of the layers above. It's a complete database toolkit.
Developers are treating PostgreSQL almost like DuckDB—an integrated query engine with a pluggable storage layer.
A world centered around object stores is a serious threat to Apache Kafka.

Kafka's primary use case is data integration—moving data between systems. But if all of your data is on an object store from start (OLTP) to finish (OLAP, search, graph, etc.) what use have you for Kafka?
A world with purpose-built, low latency, high throughput, multi-region, multi-model, transactional databases is coming; it's just a matter of time.
posts.key_insight: Database disassembly into reusable components will commoditize both performance and features, ultimately threatening Kafka's relevance as OLTP and OLAP systems converge on shared object storage.

posts.summary: This newsletter covers three distinct topics: SQLite's nuanced type system with its storage classes, datatypes, and column affinities; the evolution of durable execution and workflow orchestration interfaces from GUIs to code-based SDKs; and HikariCP's impressive JDBC connection pool performance. Riccomini explains how SQLite's type system uses text-matching rules to determine column affinities, leading to sometimes surprising but generally practical behavior. He observes that durable execution frameworks are converging with workflow orchestration tools, with each adopting multiple interface paradigms to serve different users. The piece concludes with appreciation for HikariCP's meticulous performance optimization documentation.
key_points: SQLite's type system comprises three layers: datatypes (disk format), storage classes (memory representation), and column affinities (coercion rules), with affinity determined by string matching in column type declarations
Durable execution and workflow orchestration tools are converging, with systems increasingly offering multiple interfaces: GUIs, config files, config-as-code, and full SDKs
Code-based APIs and SDKs are preferred for durable execution because they handle dynamic branching and loops better than rigid workflow definitions
HikariCP exemplifies extreme performance optimization in Java connection pooling, with documentation that serves as a masterclass in low-level optimization
money_quotes: SQLite ignores column types (unless STRICT is used). A value of any type can be written to any column.
If the declared type for a column contains any of the strings 'REAL', 'FLOA', or 'DOUB' then the column has REAL affinity.
APIs and SDKs work better for dynamic execution with branching and loops.
As durable execution systems move up the stack and workflow systems move down the stack, their addressable market (and competitors) will increase.
These different models exist because they are well suited for different users and use cases.
Their Down the Rabbit Hole post is a must-read for anyone fixated on Gunnar Morling's 1brc challenge.
posts.key_insight: SQLite's flexible type system and the convergence of durable execution with workflow orchestration both reflect the same engineering truth: different users need different interfaces to the same underlying capabilities.

posts.summary: Riccomini proposes that databases should accept Substrait query plans directly instead of text-based SQL, arguing this would eliminate an unnecessary translation layer. Currently, ORMs and query builders generate SQL text that databases must parse back into an intermediate representation—a roundabout process. By allowing applications to send structured query plans directly, databases could support multiple query languages, eliminate parsing overhead, and make programmatic access a first-class citizen. While this approach is gaining traction in data warehousing (DuckDB, Spark Connect), the OLTP space has surprisingly little adoption, presenting an opportunity for someone to build PostgreSQL support.
key_points: SQL is designed for humans to write, but machines communicating with databases would be better served by structured query formats
The current ORM-to-SQL-to-IR pipeline involves unnecessary serialization to text and back
Substrait adoption would enable databases to support multiple query frontends (SQL, PRQL, Malloy, natural language)
Data warehousing tools like DuckDB and Spark Connect already support direct query plan interfaces
The OLTP database ecosystem has surprisingly ignored this approach despite ORMs being a natural fit
money_quotes: SQL is built for humans. Machines are much better at generating structured messages.
Under the hood, query builders are all generating text SQL right now. I've always found this strange.
ORMs and query builders should be able to go straight to the IR.
Programmatic database access would become a first-class citizen.
It's silly to have applications generate text-based SQL; they should be allowed to pass query plans to the database.
Imagine if PostgreSQL had a from_substrait() extension.
Surprisingly, I haven't found any support for substrait in the OLTP space.
posts.key_insight: The translation from programmatic query builders to SQL text and back to query plans is an unnecessary indirection that Substrait could eliminate, making databases truly machine-friendly.

posts.summary: Riccomini reflects on his experience creating Apache Samza at LinkedIn, explaining how the project's architectural decisions—particularly its excessive pluggability and dependency on dynamic deployment systems like YARN—hampered adoption despite technical merits. The challenges with Samza led to internal proposals that ultimately influenced the creation of Kafka Streams and Kafka Connect as separate, purpose-built tools. He argues that stream processing has three distinct use cases (production features, analytical queries, and ETL) with contradictory requirements, and that trying to serve all of them with one monolithic platform creates unnecessary complexity. This historical perspective informs his skepticism of Apache Flink, which he sees as repeating Samza's mistakes by layering on features to become an everything-platform. He advocates for smaller, purpose-built systems that can be swapped out independently when better alternatives emerge.
key_points: Samza's pluggability and YARN dependency created onboarding friction that slowed adoption despite technical innovations in stateful stream processing
Stream processing has three distinct use cases—production features, analytical queries, and ETL—each with contradictory requirements that a single platform struggles to serve well
The internal proposals to simplify Samza directly influenced the creation of Kafka Streams and Kafka Connect as separate, focused tools
Flink has repeated Samza's mistake by becoming an everything-platform, adding orchestrators, SQL, checkpointing, and even a data lake platform at the cost of simplicity
Purpose-built systems allow you to swap out components independently—if you don't like ksqlDB, use Materialize or RisingWave instead
money_quotes: State is the hardest part of stream processing.
Literally every part was pluggable. This was great for LinkedIn because we had proprietary implementations for many of these layers. For open source users it was a rats nest of configuration.
We preferred smaller, purpose-built systems rather than one giant monolithic platform.
As for Flink, I have to ask, what is Flink for? The answer seems to be, 'It's for everything!'
Flink is just buzzier.
Privately, Flink developers admit to me that even they think it's too complicated.

You could interpret Confluent's Immerok acquisition as a capitulation to monolithic platform architecture. Or you could interpret it as a sign that customers with deep pockets fell into the one-platform trap and now have to pay someone for help.
posts.key_insight: Monolithic stream processing platforms that try to serve every use case sacrifice simplicity and user experience, while purpose-built systems for specific use cases offer easier adoption and the flexibility to swap components as better alternatives emerge.

posts.summary: This newsletter covers three main developments in data infrastructure: Apache Arrow's addition of new formats (StringView, ListView, REE) to improve interoperability with Meta's Velox execution engine, the emerging edge database ecosystem with players like Ditto, SKDB, and ElectricSQL, and a UUID performance benchmark comparing storage strategies in PostgreSQL. Riccomini expresses enthusiasm for Arrow and its ecosystem while questioning the necessity of Velox given DataFusion's broader functionality. He highlights ElectricSQL's achievement of running PostgreSQL in the browser as particularly noteworthy for simplifying client-server schema synchronization. The piece concludes with a personal anecdote about UUID storage mistakes and excitement about UUID v7's inclusion in PostgreSQL 17.
key_points: Arrow's new StringView format uses indirection to improve memory locality by encoding small strings directly in the view layer, enabling better interoperability with Meta's Velox
DataFusion appears to be a superset of Velox's functionality, making Velox most useful only for teams replacing execution engines in existing query systems like Presto or Spark
Edge databases are gaining momentum with Ditto (resilient networking via BLE/WiFi/LAN), SKDB (Firebase integration), and ElectricSQL (PostgreSQL in the browser)
Lexicographically sortable UUIDs (v7, ULIDs, Snowflake IDs) solve B-tree index performance problems caused by random insertion patterns
Arrow and its subprojects could be as impactful as Hadoop was a decade ago in reshaping database architecture
money_quotes: I believe they will be as impactful as Hadoop was a decade ago.
Frankly, I see little reason to use Velox.
DataFusion is a superset of Velox's functionality.
ElectricSQL with PostgreSQL on both the client and server simplifies a lot.
This ended up being a bad idea for a variety of reasons; it caused performance and ETL problems.
Non-time-ordered UUIDs values generated in succession are not sequential. The randomly generated values will not be clustered to each other in a database index.
I'm excited about UUID v7.
posts.key_insight: Arrow's ecosystem (DataFusion, Ballista, Comet) is positioned to become the dominant composable database infrastructure, making alternatives like Velox largely redundant except for specific migration use cases.

posts.summary: Chris Riccomini argues that databases are becoming commoditized as they converge on shared components: PostgreSQL-compatible frontends, object stores like S3 for storage, and formats like Parquet. Rather than viewing this as the death of innovation, he identifies three strategies for differentiation: building comprehensive platforms with features like branching and time travel, targeting specific business verticals like finance or SaaS, and developing multi-model systems that collapse multiple access patterns into fewer systems. He's optimistic that these strategies can combine to create compelling products, and that recent advances in decomposable data systems make previously challenging architectures newly feasible.
key_points: Database commoditization is driven by convergence on PostgreSQL protocols, object stores, and open formats like Parquet
Database platforms can differentiate through developer experience features: schema migrations, branching, time travel, built-in CDC, and ORM integration
Vertical databases like Nile (SaaS) and TigerBeetle (finance) can provide purpose-built features and data models for specific industries
Multi-model databases that collapse OLTP, OLAP, graph, search, and caching into fewer systems could fundamentally improve application architectures
Decomposable data systems (DataFusion, Velox, Substrait) and improving object stores make previously difficult architectures newly feasible
money_quotes: If every database is a PostgreSQL-compatible frontend built atop the same set of open source components, how are these databases going to compete?
Multi-model databases fundamentally change application architectures for the better.
A world with a bunch of purpose-built PostgreSQL compatible databases is only incrementally better. Developers must still adopt a different database and vendor for each access pattern.

Many developers are skeptical of HTAP databases; the industry is littered with failures. Multi-model systems look even more challenging; they try to collapse yet more access patterns. This is a feature, not a bug, though.
There's no winner for this type of database yet, so there's room to compete. And users do want this, they just can't get it (yet).
Even if every platform uses the same database, there is ample space to compete on the surrounding ecosystem.
posts.key_insight: Database commoditization isn't the end of innovation—it's the beginning of competition on platforms, verticals, and multi-model architectures that deliver value beyond raw query performance.

posts.summary: This newsletter edition covers four major developments in data infrastructure. Antithesis has launched a deterministic simulation testing platform that works with any software by using a custom hypervisor, eliminating the need to rewrite code for determinism. Polar Signals has added distributed call trace support to Parca, unifying profiling and tracing in one system. Google Cloud Storage is responding to AWS S3 Express One Zone by planning 100x latency improvements. The post concludes with a highlight of CozoDB, an embedded database combining graph, vector, and relational capabilities with Datalog queries.
key_points: Antithesis solves deterministic simulation testing for legacy code by emulating entire machines at the hypervisor level, removing the need to rewrite non-deterministic software
Polar Signals now combines distributed call tracing with CPU profiling, allowing developers to trace performance issues across entire microservice request chains
Google Cloud Storage is planning 1-2 orders of magnitude latency improvements to compete with AWS S3 Express One Zone
Low-latency object stores with optimistic concurrency control will fundamentally change how distributed data systems are built, potentially eliminating complex control planes
CozoDB combines graph, vector, and relational database paradigms with pluggable storage backends including distributed options like TiKV
money_quotes: Once software is deterministic it's much easier to test. And a test that fails once will always fail—no more flaky tests!
Antithesis solved this problem by writing a custom hypervisor that emulates an entire machine. Software no longer needs to be deterministic in order to do deterministic simulation testing.
When failures happen, Antithesis can play back every instruction—not just the application instructions, but all of the operating system ones as well.

Developers use distributed call tracing to figure out which microservices are causing the most latency for the frontend call. Once the bottleneck is found, a different set of tools—profilers—is used to figure out why the service is slow.
An object store with such characteristics will completely change how we build data systems.
The hard parts of distributed data infrastructure will be managed by the object store.

Graphs can be thought of as nearest-neighbor search where edges define explicit links to nearest neighbors. Conversely, nearest neighbor search can be thought of as graph traversal, where nearest neighbors define implicit edges in the graph.
posts.key_insight: Infrastructure tooling is converging—simulation testing is becoming accessible to legacy systems, profiling and tracing are merging, and low-latency object stores may soon absorb the complexity of distributed data systems.

posts.summary: This interview explores ElectricSQL, an open-source platform for building local-first software that aims to eliminate the complexity of state transfer between frontend and backend. CEO James Arthur explains how ElectricSQL provides conflict-free and rollback-free programming through Transactional Causal Consistency with CRDTs, allowing developers to write to local databases without coding conflict or sync handlers. The discussion covers schema evolution challenges, where ElectricSQL only supports additive migrations to maintain finality of local writes. Arthur also discusses their choice of Elixir for the sync service, leveraging the BEAM runtime for fault tolerance and concurrency, and introduces PGLite as a future option for unified client-server data models.
key_points: ElectricSQL targets the state transfer layer, aiming to eliminate boilerplate code for serialization, caching, state management, and reactivity by making data live and reactive
The platform provides 'Finality of Local Writes' - local writes are never rejected by the server, eliminating rollbacks and making local-first development viable for mainstream applications
Schema evolution is handled through additive-only migrations, ensuring stale writes from offline clients remain valid against evolved schemas
Elixir and the BEAM runtime were chosen for the sync service due to their natural support for fault tolerance, high concurrency, and efficient stream processing
PGLite offers the prospect of unified client-server data models, enabling advanced features like pgvector embeddings and PostGIS to work identically on both ends
money_quotes: So much of web and mobile development is about ferrying data back and forth and coding across the network that the choice tends to dominate the architecture of your app and the code you have to write.
We see local-first as the best way of building pretty much all software in the future.
The delight and the promise of local-first is to be able to reduce the complexity of the development stack to just your data and your components.

Writes made to the local embedded database in the client are not subject to being rejected by the server. This gets rid of rollbacks and tentativity, which is a key advance in simplifying the local-first programming model.
Whilst the software architecture orthodoxy emphasizes the cons of tight coupling, there genuinely are both pros and cons here.
If you're building consumer, prosumer, or SaaS software and you're not building local-first, ask yourself how your product is going to compare to a product that is.
Do you want your product to feel old and laggy like Jira or super snappy and modern, like Linear and Superhuman?
posts.key_insight: Local-first architecture with conflict-free replicated data types can eliminate the state transfer complexity that dominates modern app development, but requires accepting trade-offs in schema evolution and embracing tighter coupling between components.

posts.summary: Apache Kafka has evolved from an open source project into a de facto protocol, with multiple vendors building their own implementations that diverge significantly from the original Apache codebase. Confluent Cloud's features like tiered storage and Iceberg integration are years ahead of the Apache project, and other implementations like WarpStream, Redpanda, AutoMQ, and S2 are taking radically different approaches while maintaining Kafka protocol compatibility. This pattern mirrors what happened with Redis and PostgreSQL protocols, and even S3. The author argues this is a natural and healthy evolution for successful infrastructure, though it raises questions about companies' responsibilities to their open source roots.
key_points: Confluent Cloud's feature set has diverged so much from Apache Kafka that it's nearly unrecognizable—tiered storage arrived in Confluent 4+ years before Apache Kafka
Multiple vendors (WarpStream, Redpanda, AutoMQ, S2, StreamNative) now implement the Kafka protocol with completely different architectures
Successful infrastructure is destined to become a protocol, following the pattern of Redis, PostgreSQL, and S3
Pure open source business models à la Hortonworks are no longer viable
Companies often choose strategic ambiguity over explicitly acknowledging their divergence from open source roots
money_quotes: Kafka is now a protocol, not an Apache project. The Apache project is just one (aging) implementation.
Confluent Cloud's feature set has so diverged from Apache Kafka that it's nearly unrecognizable.
Successful infrastructure is destined to be a protocol.
I don't see a pure open source business—à la Hortonworks—as a viable model.
Users are going to have to get comfortable inferring a company's intentions based on their actions.
All of this begs the question: what responsibility—if any—do companies have to their open source roots, or to the protocol itself?
They worry—rightly so—that they'll alienate users and suffer brand damage if they explicitly abandon their open source roots. So they opt for strategic ambiguity.
posts.key_insight: When infrastructure becomes successful enough, the protocol transcends any single implementation—and the open source project becomes just one option among many.

posts.summary: Chris Riccomini returns from a break to discuss his renewed attempt at learning Rust, reflecting on the database commoditization conversation sparked by his previous post and Jack Vanlightly's thoughtful follow-up. He describes his 2022 failure with Rust during Advent of Code and how GPT-4 and Gemini have made his current learning experience dramatically better. While he appreciates Rust's mature ecosystem and excellent documentation, he criticizes its thin standard library that requires external packages for basic functionality. He contrasts Rust with Zig, noting that despite preferring Zig's philosophy, its immature ecosystem and questionable package management decisions make it harder to adopt.
key_points: Jack Vanlightly's follow-up argues that platforms—coherent sets of features greater than the sum of their parts—could help infrastructure vendors differentiate beyond commoditization
LLMs like GPT-4 have transformed the experience of learning new programming languages, acting as senior engineers for specific technical questions
Rust has a thin standard library philosophy that requires external packages for basic functionality like async runtimes and random numbers, leading to dependency bloat similar to node_modules
Ecosystems matter more than languages—Rust's mature tooling (Cargo, documentation, libraries) gives it a significant advantage over younger alternatives like Zig
Zig's new package management approach requiring build.zig files feels like a step backward, triggering 'sbt and build.gradle trauma'
money_quotes: I'm really annoyed that you all are making me.
How do you know if someone writes Rust? They will tell you.
Rust is the opposite of what I want; it's a thick language with a thin standard library.
People have been making jokes about node_modules for a decade now, but this problem is just as bad in Rust codebases I've seen.
Focus on debugging your application rather than debugging your programming language knowledge.
Ecosystems are more important than languages to me.
A platform is a coherent and cohesive set of features that seamlessly work together to provide a service more than the sum of its parts.
posts.key_insight: Despite philosophical preferences for alternatives like Zig, ecosystem maturity and tooling quality ultimately matter more than language design when choosing systems programming languages.

posts.summary: Riccomini examines two competing architectural approaches for PostgreSQL-compatible databases: building extensions on top of PostgreSQL's query engine versus implementing protocol compatibility while building a new engine from scratch. He argues against the view that PostgreSQL is a 'near-perfect kernel,' noting that it constrains what you can build. With maturing components like DataFusion, Velox, and RocksDB, building custom databases is more feasible than ever. He predicts PostgreSQL's protocol, not its query engine, will be its most defining characteristic, while acknowledging both approaches will coexist in the market.
key_points: Two architectural camps are emerging: PostgreSQL extension vendors (Hydra, Tembo, ParadeDB, Neon) versus protocol-compatible custom engines (CedarDB, CockroachDB, Yugabyte, Aurora)
Building your own database is no longer as daunting due to maturing components like DataFusion, Substrait, Velox, Parquet, and RocksDB
The big three cloud providers have limited extension support, creating opportunities for third-party vendors and multi-model databases
New databases will need to be multi-model to justify adoption costs by amortizing them across multiple use cases
PostgreSQL as a kernel is more limiting than building your own—the more control you want, the more it feels like a straight jacket
money_quotes: Tying yourself to PostgreSQL is always going to be more limiting than building your own database. You're limited by what PostgreSQL can offer you.
The more control you want, the more PostgreSQL is going to feel like a straight jacket.
Building your own database doesn't sound as crazy as it used to.
The question is not whether to package or fork PostgreSQL, but whether to extend PostgreSQL using the extensions API or the protocol.
My gut says that PostgreSQL's protocol, not its query engine, will be its most defining characteristic.
Multi-model databases amortize the adoption cost across multiple use cases.
New infrastructure must justify its upfront adoption cost.
posts.key_insight: PostgreSQL's wire protocol and SQL dialect—not its query engine—will ultimately be its most valuable and enduring contribution to the database ecosystem.

posts.summary: Chris Riccomini argues that object storage (S3, GCS) is becoming the future of database persistence, but current systems require write caches due to latency and atomicity limitations. He introduces the 'LCD theorem'—a tradeoff between Latency, Cost, and Durability when persisting directly to object storage. With improvements like S3 Express and compare-and-swap operations becoming standard, he believes write caches will become unnecessary. He's building SlateDB, a cloud-native LSM tree database that persists directly to object storage, using write batching to manage costs while letting clients choose their tradeoff between waiting for durability or accepting async writes.
key_points: Object storage improvements (S3 Express, CAS/preconditions) are eliminating the need for separate write caches in front of object stores
Direct object storage writes face a cost problem: 10K writes/second at S3 pricing would cost $65K/month without batching
Write batching (10-100ms windows) decouples client write rate from PUT costs, reducing the same workload to $650/month
The LCD theorem presents a clear tradeoff: clients must choose between low latency, low cost, and high durability—they can't have all three
SlateDB is an experimental Rust LSM tree that persists WAL directly to object storage, letting clients configure batch windows similar to Kafka's linger.ms
money_quotes: I believe that the future of database persistence is object storage—S3, Google Cloud Storage, and so on.
I don't think we'll need these write caches in the future. Object storage is changing fast.
A high-volume service that sustains 10,000 writes per-second would cost 2.5c per-second, or $65,000 per-month.
Batching decouples the client write-rate from the PUT calls sent to object storage.
This triad of latency, cost, and durability presents an easy mental model; something I'm facetiously calling the latency, cost, durability (LCD) theorem.
Clients that want low latency and high durability have to pay.
LCD-style systems have a rather elegant design.
posts.key_insight: As object storage gains low latency and atomic operations, databases can persist directly to it by batching writes—but clients must explicitly choose between latency, cost, and durability.

posts.summary: The author reflects on Swift's memory management approach compared to Rust's borrow checker after a friend suggested Rust should have adopted Swift's ARC model. Investigation reveals that Apple published an 'Ownership Manifesto' seven years ago proposing Rust-like ownership for Swift, and this work has now materialized in Swift 5.9 with noncopyable types, consume operators, and borrowing/consuming parameter modifiers. The author expresses ambivalence about these changes, appreciating Swift's simpler ARC model while acknowledging that deep infrastructure work requires the performance optimizations that ownership semantics provide.
key_points: Swift uses automatic reference counting (ARC) which is generally fast and works well, though it has edge cases like cyclic references and performance overhead from reference counting
Apple's 'Ownership Manifesto' proposing Rust-like ownership was posted over 7 years ago, showing this direction has been long planned
Swift 5.9 introduces noncopyable structs/enums, consume operators, and borrowing/consuming parameter ownership modifiers
The tension exists between Swift's simpler memory model and the performance requirements of deep infrastructure development
Some in the community see Swift as a viable 'plan B' if Rust declines in popularity
money_quotes: Infrastructure developers could just get over themselves.
Reference counting provides deterministic performance in most cases, but that performance can still be complex to analyze and predict.
For current Rust users, if Rust kicks the bucket, Swift is a good plan B.
I'm torn on these changes. On the one hand, I like Swift's relatively simple memory management. On the other hand, I buy the argument that ARC can be slow in certain circumstances.
Some of ARC's edge cases happen to matter a lot when building deep infrastructure—the kind of stuff you build with Rust.
Perhaps Swift has managed to thread the needle with their ownership semantics in a way that Rust hasn't.
posts.key_insight: Swift's evolution toward Rust-like ownership semantics in version 5.9 suggests that even languages with simpler memory models eventually need more explicit control for performance-critical infrastructure work.

posts.summary: Chris Riccomini examines two new columnar file formats—Nimble from Meta and Lance V2 from LanceDB—that aim to address Apache Parquet's limitations for AI/ML workloads. Parquet struggles with point lookups, large values, wide schemas, and limited encodings that make it poorly suited for machine learning data with long float arrays and multimedia content. Nimble takes an incremental approach with extensible encodings and FlatBuffers for metadata, while LV2 radically eliminates row groups entirely in favor of a minimal ~200 line spec with pluggable type systems. Both formats prioritize extensibility, though this risks fragmentation across implementations. The author concludes that while neither format is production-ready for general OLAP queries, LV2's integration with Arrow and thoughtful design make it particularly compelling for the future.
key_points: Parquet's row group architecture creates problems for ML workloads: expensive point lookups, difficulty handling large values like feature vectors, and poor support for wide schemas with thousands of columns

Nimble maintains stripes but adds extensible encodings, FlatBuffers for selective metadata decoding, and flexible metadata storage—Meta explicitly discourages reimplementations to prevent fragmentation
Lance V2 completely eliminates row groups, reducing the spec to ~200 lines of Protobuf with no built-in type system or encodings—everything is pluggable
Extensibility is a double-edged sword: it enables innovation but causes fragmentation when different teams use encodings their colleagues' libraries don't support
LV2's unstated philosophy is that Arrow will dictate standards for types and encodings, which provides a practical path through the extensibility challenge
money_quotes: Engineers love to make things extensible. It's an escape hatch for bad decisions, the promise of flexibility, and an openness to new ideas. But extensibility causes fragmentation.
They're dreaming. It's not written in Rust, so that's going to happen. There will be others, too. Engineers just can't resist reimplementing specs in their native languages.
LV2 is so thin, the whole spec is a mere ~200 line Protobuf file.
And we're just talking about encodings. LV2's choice to ditch both encodings and type systems really freaked me out.
I've since realized that LV2 has adopted an unstated philosophy: Arrow will dictate the standard.
This is hell for data engineers. At least, that was my first reaction.
Given all this, it's obvious that the formats are not ready for prime-time yet. Most analytical use cases are still best suited for Parquet.
posts.key_insight: While Parquet's limitations for AI/ML workloads have spawned promising new formats in Nimble and Lance V2, their embrace of extensibility trades flexibility for fragmentation risk—though LV2's implicit reliance on Arrow as the de facto standard offers a pragmatic path forward.

posts.summary: Chris Riccomini argues that S3, despite being an engineering marvel, is falling behind competitors due to missing critical features like compare-and-swap operations, multi-region buckets, and object appends. These gaps are becoming problematic as new infrastructure increasingly uses object storage as their primary persistence layer. S3 Express One Zone has proven disappointing with its directory bucket type, missing standard S3 features, and high storage costs. Developers face a choice: abandon S3 for competitors like GCS that have these features, or build workarounds using additional services like DynamoDB. The post concludes that understanding S3 as an object store rather than a file system, and embracing a separate metadata layer, may be the path forward.
key_points: S3 is the only major object store without preconditions/compare-and-swap operations, forcing developers to use DynamoDB for transactional operations
S3 Express One Zone is underwhelming: twice the cost of EBS, missing standard S3 features, and requires manual quorum writes for availability
Unlike GCS, S3 lacks dual-region and multi-region bucket options for higher availability
Some companies like Turbopuffer are abandoning S3 entirely, betting that AWS will eventually add preconditions
The pragmatic path is recognizing S3 as an object store, not a file system, and embracing DynamoDB as a metadata layer
money_quotes: I'm squarely in the trough of disillusionment with S3.
S3 is the only object store that doesn't support preconditions.
Building a two-phase write between DynamoDB and S3 is not technically difficult, but it's annoying and leads to ugly abstractions.
S3E1Z looks more like an expensive EBS with a half-implemented S3 API.
The dream is that developers are offered an object store with all of these features: low latency, preconditions, dual-region/multi-region, and so on. But we live in reality.
The path to the slope of enlightenment is to recognize that S3 is an object store not a file system.
posts.key_insight: S3's missing features like compare-and-swap operations are forcing developers to either abandon AWS's object store for competitors or architect around its limitations with additional metadata layers.

posts.summary: Riccomini critiques Google Cloud's handling of a major outage that deleted UniSuper's private cloud for over a week due to a misconfigured parameter. He argues the incident reveals three systemic problems: Google's fundamental misunderstanding of enterprise customers, their superficial incident remediation that only addressed the proximate cause, and growing brand damage as Google Cloud gains a reputation for instability. Despite being a self-professed Google Cloud fan, he finds their incident response tone-deaf—notably lacking any apology while defensively claiming to be 'the most resilient cloud infrastructure in the world.'
key_points: Google's incident response contained no apology and defensively emphasized the outage only affected one customer, revealing a fundamental disconnect with enterprise customer expectations
The remediation was dangerously superficial—only addressing the specific deletion flow rather than auditing other tools, products, and deletion workflows across the organization
Enterprise customers expect staged deletions before hard-deleting anything, and more than a 'terse single-page remediation'
Google Cloud is developing a reputation for instability, with even Google's own Waymo reportedly not using it for infrastructure
Google historically ran their cloud product like consumer software, even A/B testing admin interfaces during production outages
money_quotes: It's important to talk about a company's failures as well as their successes.
Nowhere does Google's post even apologize. In fact, the only apology I can find is on UniSuper's website.
While this may be true, I find it completely tone-deaf. So much for customer empathy.
Enterprises expect more than a terse single-page remediation with no apology.
You don't want to A/B test user interfaces in an organization when they're in the middle of a production outage.
Google could have audited their other deletion flows, their other tools, and their other products. They could have modified deletion workflows to stage deletions. Instead, they did none of this. Why?
Google's remediation indicates that they don't understand how damaging this outage was to their brand.
If Google won't use their cloud service, what business do you have selling it to others?
posts.key_insight: Google Cloud's dismissive incident response and superficial remediation reveal a company that still treats enterprise customers like consumer software users, failing to understand that trust—not just technical capability—is what enterprises are buying.

posts.summary: Chris Riccomini argues that deterministic simulation testing (DST) is gaining momentum, with Antithesis's hypervisor-based approach revolutionizing the field by making any containerized code deterministically testable without modification. However, Antithesis faces adoption challenges due to high costs (~$200k) and integration complexity. Riccomini predicts open source alternatives will inevitably emerge, citing projects like Meta's Hermit and QEMU-based efforts. He advocates that Antithesis should open source their hypervisor since most of their value lies in surrounding tooling, and community development could accelerate integration improvements. The post concludes optimistically with Antithesis confirming they plan to eventually open source their hypervisor.
key_points: Deterministic simulation testing traditionally required developers to write deterministic code themselves, managing clocks, random numbers, and process scheduling—a significant burden
Antithesis's hypervisor-based approach is groundbreaking because it makes entire containers deterministic without code modification, enabling DST for the masses
High costs (~$200k) and integration complexity are limiting Antithesis's adoption, particularly for early-stage startups
Open source alternatives are inevitable—multiple projects (Hermit, QEMU modifications, Go runtime changes) are already pursuing deterministic execution
Antithesis's real value lies not in the hypervisor itself but in surrounding tools like AWS mocking, Software Explorer, and debugging features
money_quotes: Antithesis's design is ground breaking. Developers can now take any containerized code and run it deterministically on Antithesis. DST for the masses.
There's no doubt in my mind that their product is worth well north of $200,000 for a large company selling infrastructure to hundreds of customers. For early stage startups, it's a tough sell.
Building a non-deterministic hypervisor is just too juicy of a problem. There will be open source implementations.
Yes, it's a novel innovation, but it seems inevitable that there will be open source implementations.
Most of Antithesis's value isn't in their hypervisor anyway.
I'm very excited to hear it's on their minds, and they're planning to open up their hypervisor.
posts.key_insight: Antithesis should open source their deterministic hypervisor because the real value lies in their surrounding tooling, and community development would accelerate the integration improvements needed for broader adoption.

posts.summary: Chris Riccomini explores three interconnected topics in data infrastructure. First, he celebrates ParadeDB's pg_lakehouse extension, which turns PostgreSQL into a data lake query engine using Apache DataFusion and OpenDAL. Second, he examines approaches for serving lakehouse data back to production systems, favoring simple caching services like ROAPI over heavier databases like StarRocks or SingleStore. Finally, he discusses the aging Kafka Connect platform and the need for improvements around connector isolation and scheduling, noting that competitors like Bento and Redpanda Connect are presenting healthy alternatives.
key_points: pg_lakehouse demonstrates that composable data systems built on reusable components like DataFusion and OpenDAL are not just theoretical but already shipping
Serving analytics data back to production requires a caching layer in front of data lakes—something ROAPI lacks but databases like Rockset and StarRocks provide
The pattern of pre-computing data in warehouses and pushing to production remains unchanged since LinkedIn's Hadoop-to-Voldemort days, only the systems have evolved
Kafka Connect is showing its age, with teams at Twitter and WePay having to bypass its built-in cluster management and HTTP API to operate at scale
Competition from Bento and Redpanda Connect is healthy for the connector ecosystem, even if standardization would be ideal
