money_quotes: pg_lakehouse is proof that composable data systems are not only possible, but are already here.
Systems have changed but the pattern remains the same.
What I want is a cache that sits in front of warehouse data and exposes it through a low-latency API.
The 'Connect-as-a-Service' part of Connect was completely absent.
We had to dumb-down Connect's cluster management features and bypass the HTTP API in order to achieve our goals.
I do think it's healthy for competitors like Bento and Redpanda Connect to present alternatives.
posts.key_insight: The future of data infrastructure lies in composable systems built from reusable components like DataFusion, while established platforms like Kafka Connect need modernization to remain relevant.

posts.summary: Data lake catalogs have become a battleground as Snowflake launched Polaris, Databricks open-sourced Unity catalog, and Databricks acquired Tabular. Unlike traditional data catalogs focused on discovery and governance, these new catalogs serve as an information_schema for query engines like Flink, Spark, and Trino. Table formats alone are insufficient—catalogs provide the crucial layer that tracks all databases, tables, and partitions. The strategic importance lies in catalogs being the integration point for query engines, making them a natural stepping stone for vendors to move up or down the stack to protect their positions.
key_points: Data lake catalogs serve a different purpose than traditional data catalogs—they're information_schema for query engines rather than discovery tools for analysts
Table formats (Iceberg, Hudi, Delta) are necessary but insufficient; catalogs sit above them and provide the entry point query engines need
Building companies around table formats alone seemed odd—like building around Parquet or ORC—which explains the upward movement into catalogs
A dominant catalog company could threaten existing vendors by moving up into the query engine layer, prompting defensive consolidation
The composable data stack has catalogs positioned between query engines above and table formats below
money_quotes: Table formats like Iceberg, Hudi, and Delta always struck me as an odd layer for companies to fight over.
The formats themselves are specifications that define a file, folder, and byte structure in a filesystem. It felt akin to building companies around storage formats like Parquet or ORC.
The layer above the table format—the catalog—is the more important layer; it's where query engines integrate.
An integrated catalog, table format, and file format is compelling. Such a product contains the entry point to the data plane and much of the data plane itself.
A dominant catalog company could represent a significant threat if the company chose to move up the stack by offering or even favoring a query engine.
Another vendor taking control of the data layer could be quite disruptive. So we see vendors moving down the stack to shore up their product offerings.
posts.key_insight: Catalogs, not table formats, are the strategic control point in the data lakehouse stack because they're where query engines integrate, making them the natural battleground for vendor consolidation.

posts.summary: Riccomini examines the recent open source catalog announcements from Databricks (Unity) and Snowflake (Polaris), finding both fall far short of their marketing promises. Snowflake's Polaris Github repository is essentially empty despite extensive marketing materials. Databricks released an API-compatible rewrite of Unity rather than their actual product, missing critical features like audit capabilities and ML/AI governance. A third-party Rust rewrite (unity-rs) has also emerged, but it's barely started. Riccomini concludes that while these projects will likely mature, the vendors appear to be flailing without a clear strategy.
key_points: Snowflake's Polaris catalog was announced with significant marketing but the Github repository contains no actual code yet
Databricks open sourced an API-compatible rewrite of Unity rather than their production system, missing key features like audit capabilities and external storage support
A third-party Rust rewrite (unity-rs) has emerged but is essentially just a hello world file
Despite the challenges, extracting internal projects is genuinely difficult and rewrites are often the right approach
The vendors appear to lack a clear, articulated strategy for why they're open sourcing these catalogs
money_quotes: Yes, but have you looked at the code?
Out of all of these announcements, all this marketing, and all this noise, we've gotten one partial re-write, one empty Github project, and one hello world Rust file.
It appears the vendors have gotten ahead of themselves.
I'm not even sure they all understand why they're open sourcing these projects.
I'd love to see someone write down their data lakehouse strategy, or at least do a better job of communicating what the end state of all this is.
Right now it looks like flailing.
It's often hard to extricate internal projects. A rewrite is often the right move.
posts.key_insight: The gap between vendor marketing and actual open source deliverables in the data lakehouse catalog space suggests these companies are rushing to stake claims without clear strategies or substantial code to back up their announcements.

posts.summary: Chris Riccomini argues that the data lakehouse ecosystem should standardize on Apache Iceberg's REST API for core catalog functionality, with query engines like Trino and DataFusion driving its evolution rather than individual vendors. He distinguishes between 'core' functionality (metadata for query execution) and 'non-core' features (governance, lineage, discovery), suggesting vendors should compete on the latter. The post notes that major players like Databricks Unity, Snowflake Polaris, and Nessie are already adopting Iceberg's REST API. This standardization would allow query engines to deprecate fragmented integrations like Hive Metastore and AWS Glue catalog, creating a cleaner ecosystem.
key_points: Core catalog functionality (query metadata) should standardize on Iceberg's REST API, while vendors compete on non-core features like governance and discovery
Query engines like Trino and DataFusion should drive the API's evolution, reducing control by any single vendor like Databricks or Snowflake
Major vendors are already converging on Iceberg REST: Databricks Unity, Snowflake Polaris, Nessie, and Apache Gravitino
Data lakehouse catalogs have found the 'must-have' wedge that traditional data catalogs like Alation and Datahub have struggled to achieve
LanceDB's approach of deferring to Arrow for types and encodings is a model for how the catalog space should defer to query engine needs
money_quotes: I would love to see everyone congregate around Apache Iceberg's REST API for core lakehouse catalog functionality.
I'd like to see query engines take a more active role in evolving the spec, particularly Apache DataFusion and Trino.
Reorienting the API evolution around the query engines it serves means more projects can take an active role, thereby reducing the control of one or two vendors.
Don't mistake 'non-core' for not valuable. Much of the functionality in this bucket is extremely valuable, if not required for enterprises.
Data lakehouse vendors have accidentally stumbled upon the wedge that traditional data catalogs have been struggling to find.
Traditional data catalogs have been unable to shift from nice-to-have to must-have in the enterprise; they remain a dusty tool that's often ignored once installed.
Data lakehouse catalogs are must-have for anyone running query engines on a data lake.
posts.key_insight: The lakehouse ecosystem should let query engines drive catalog API evolution while vendors compete on enterprise features, turning infrastructure standards into a rising tide that lifts all boats.

posts.summary: This interview explores the emerging Bring Your Own Cloud (BYOC) deployment model as an alternative to traditional multi-tenant SaaS. Jon Morehouse argues that B2B software vendors have unnecessarily adopted B2C multi-tenant architectures that many enterprise customers don't actually want, as customers increasingly value data ownership over convenience. BYOC decouples software management from deployment, allowing vendors to manage software that runs in customer environments. The conversation covers practical challenges (installation automation, day-2 tooling, updates) that Nuon aims to solve, and distinguishes between 'vertical clouds' like Snowflake/Confluent and application vendors who should embrace BYOC. Morehouse envisions a future where B2B SaaS vendors only offer BYOC, fundamentally changing SaaS margins to purely R&D and support costs.
key_points: Traditional SaaS coupled deployment with management, creating an all-or-nothing dynamic that enterprise customers increasingly reject as data becomes more valuable
BYOC allows vendors to serve their entire customer base with one deployment option instead of maintaining separate SaaS and on-premises offerings
Three main challenges for BYOC: installation automation, day-2 operational tooling without direct cloud access, and managing updates across customers with different requirements
A key distinction exists between 'vertical clouds' (Confluent, Snowflake, Databricks) who will BE the cloud in BYOC, versus application vendors who should run ON those clouds
BYOC flips the pricing negotiation power dynamic—vendors can leverage their customers' buying power rather than needing their own
money_quotes: Unless you are a vertical cloud, you probably only need to design for your largest customers.
As we look at the next 5 years, customer data is only going to increase in value.
It feels like each B2B/infrastructure company has invested all this money, time, and energy into building SaaS offerings; yet their customer's do not actually want them. It just feels… broken.
Instead of building a sophisticated SaaS multi-tenant offering that many customers don't actually want, you can focus all that energy on just building a great BYOC experience.
Imagine a world where all SaaS companies are running in their customer's cloud account. SaaS margins would be purely R&D and support.
All of today's cloud products are meant for building software, not consuming software.
We believe in a world where most software is run in customer accounts.
posts.key_insight: The future of B2B software lies in decoupling deployment from management through BYOC, allowing vendors to provide SaaS-like experiences while customers retain control of their data and infrastructure costs.

posts.summary: Chris Riccomini traces the evolution of realtime OLAP systems through his personal experience at LinkedIn, starting with the Who's Viewed My Profile feature 15 years ago. The initial solution, Avatara, used pre-materialized views stored in Voldemort but suffered from rigid query patterns, high write amplification, and data staleness. This led to the development of Apache Druid by Eric Tschetter at MetaMarkets and Apache Pinot by Kishore Gopalakrishna at LinkedIn, both solving these problems through columnar storage combined with in-memory buffering for recent data. The post explains how these systems enable millisecond-latency queries on fresh data without the limitations of pre-computation, setting the stage for a future discussion of ClickHouse.
key_points: Traditional data warehouses couldn't serve user-facing analytics due to slow response times (seconds to minutes) and lack of real-time data freshness
Avatara's pre-materialization approach created three key problems: rigid query patterns, high write amplification, and stale data
Druid and Pinot solved these problems by combining columnar storage for historical data with in-memory buffering for recent ingestion
Pinot introduced star-tree indexes to give operators a tuning knob between storage space and query latency
The fundamental challenge is serving OLAP-style aggregation queries to external users at millisecond latencies with fresh data
money_quotes: It was a classic vanity metric. Naturally, the product was popular and drove considerable subscriber revenue.
OLAP developed many fancy concepts—cubes, dimensions, measures, and so on. But it's really just SELECT COUNT(*) FROM … GROUP BY queries.

Data warehouses are built to handle queries on large data sets but with slower response times (seconds to minutes). WVMP requires millisecond response times since end-users are waiting for the page to load.
A single profile view resulted in one write (or increment) for each combination of dimensions—high write amplification.
Druid had a clever solution to the batch-write problem: store recently ingested in-memory as well.
Data could be ingested through Kafka topics in realtime because writes simply went to memory. Ingested data was immediately available for reads.
posts.key_insight: Realtime OLAP systems evolved from rigid pre-materialized views to flexible columnar stores with in-memory buffering, enabling millisecond-latency user-facing analytics without sacrificing query flexibility or data freshness.

posts.summary: In this interview, Luke Kim of Spice AI explains how his startup addresses the gap in serving data lakehouse files in production. Spice AI functions as a CDN for databases, providing a caching and materialization layer that sits between applications and data warehouses/lakes. The architecture prioritizes single-node optimization with horizontal scalability, drawing from Kim's experience building Dapr at Microsoft. Spice can be deployed as sidecars alongside applications, stackable across infrastructure tiers from edge to cloud, minimizing data transit while maintaining query performance. The system supports both proactive materialization and reactive caching, with CDC support for consistency across distributed replicas.
key_points: The data ecosystem has matured for writes and transforms (ETL/ELT), but a significant gap remains for read and query scenarios in production

Spice AI inverts the traditional application-data warehouse relationship by deploying lightweight, single-node optimized runtimes alongside each application rather than having many applications query a single cluster
The 'Big Data is Dead' thesis applies here: the working set of data queried day-to-day is typically 2-3 orders of magnitude smaller than what an application could query
Sidecars provide language/runtime separation while maintaining performance through fast kernel-level local loopback, making building blocks portable and platform agnostic
Tiered storage architecture allows Spice instances to be stacked from edge to cloud, with CDC over Arrow Flight for real-time consistency
money_quotes: The history of the technology industry is a story of turning lower layers of the application stack into commoditized building blocks.
Like the cloud infrastructure example, lower level plumbing can be slow to build, expensive, hard to maintain and get right, and often offers little differentiated business value.
Don't fight physics; minimize data transit.
The working set of data queried day-to-day is normally 2-3 orders of magnitude smaller than the data the application could query.
Spice inverts the application-data warehouse relationship to be more distributed.
Separation from the language and runtime enables common functionality to be portable and platform agnostic, while maintaining performance through a very fast kernel-level local loopback.
As you move up this stack, you want to focus on delivering user value at higher and higher levels, not deal with the lower level data plumbing.
posts.key_insight: The missing piece in the data lakehouse ecosystem is a CDN-like caching layer that brings data closer to applications, and the sidecar pattern perfected by service meshes provides the ideal deployment model for this use case.

posts.summary: Riccomini chronicles his experience building fraud detection systems at WePay, revealing how this work deepened his understanding of realtime OLAP requirements. The fraud detection use case demanded sub-10-second data freshness and single-digit millisecond query responses—far stricter than human-facing dashboards. After evaluating Druid and finding it insufficient for their latency needs, WePay built a custom pipeline using Kafka, BigQuery, Dataflow, and Bigtable. This experience led Riccomini to distinguish between 'human interaction' OLAP (flexible queries, hundreds of milliseconds acceptable) and 'machine interaction' OLAP (pre-defined queries, millisecond response times required). He observes that realtime OLAP appears across domains—fraud, observability, logging, metrics—each with overlapping but distinct requirements, and that these specialized systems are now converging.
key_points: Fraud detection models require single-digit millisecond query latency and sub-10-second data freshness—constraints that led WePay to build custom infrastructure rather than use Druid
The distinction between 'human interaction' and 'machine interaction' OLAP clarifies why different use cases demand different system architectures
Realtime OLAP patterns appear everywhere: observability dashboards, log analytics, metrics, and business intelligence are all variations on GROUP BY queries with different latency/freshness tradeoffs
The realtime OLAP landscape is converging: data warehouses are adding realtime ingest, OLAP systems are adding JOINs, and stream processors are satisfying fraud detection use cases
money_quotes: Fraudsters act quickly and often use scripts. We needed activity data to be available to the model on the order of seconds so we could detect fraudulent behavior as it was occurring.
I started calling the former 'human interaction' and the latter 'machine interaction'.

Human interaction OLAP with realtime slice-and-dice dashboards. Response times could take a few hundred milliseconds while the chart updated. Machine interaction, by contrast, had pre-defined queries but required much faster response times.
I started to see realtime OLAP everywhere.
The Kibana dashboards looked an awful lot like business analytics chart'n'graph dashboards to me. The data was just machine instances, CPUs, and disks.
Data warehouses, real-time OLAP systems, time series databases, and search engines might all converge.

We had a tight bound on the number of milliseconds that a transaction would wait for a fraud response. If the window passed, we had to decide whether to reject the transaction outright or risk allowing fraudulent transactions through.
posts.key_insight: Realtime OLAP is not a single problem but a spectrum of use cases with varying requirements for latency, freshness, and query flexibility—and the systems built for each niche are now converging.

posts.summary: Riccomini announces the open-source release of SlateDB, an embedded storage engine built as an LSM tree directly on object storage like S3. The project emerged from his earlier writing about cloud storage tradeoffs and attracted collaborators from companies like Responsive and Jam Socket. SlateDB targets use cases in stateful stream processing, serverless functions, and durable execution where bottomless storage capacity and durability matter more than raw latency. The project is explicitly non-commercial with an Apache 2.0 license and a CNCF sandbox application pending.
key_points: SlateDB is an embedded key-value storage engine that writes all data to object storage, trading latency for unlimited capacity and high durability
The project fills a gap in the ecosystem—existing solutions like RocksDB weren't purpose-built for object storage
Built collaboratively by engineers from Responsive, Azure, and Jam Socket over four months before open-sourcing
Riccomini explicitly commits to no commercial plans—just building a successful open source project under Apache 2.0
Major features still in development include caching, compaction, snapshots, garbage collection, transactions, and range queries
money_quotes: Object storage allows SlateDB to provide bottomless storage capacity, high durability, and easy replication.
We built SlateDB because we felt an open source LSM on object storage would be useful for stateful stream processing, serverless functions, durable execution, and more.
Companies like WarpStream and Turbopuffer set zero disk architecture on the map.
We couldn't find an LSM that was purpose-built for object storage. So we built it.
I am explicitly not going to do anything with SlateDB other than help make it a successful open source project.
I am not going to start a company around it or try and monetize it in any way.
posts.key_insight: The rise of object storage has created a gap for purpose-built storage engines that embrace its tradeoffs rather than treating it as a drop-in disk replacement.

posts.summary: Chris Riccomini investigates the growing enthusiasm around ClickHouse, a realtime OLAP database that has gained significant attention in the data infrastructure community. He identifies three key factors driving the buzz: exceptional speed, remarkably easy installation via a single cURL command, and operational simplicity that allows scaling up on a single machine before needing distributed coordination. However, Riccomini highlights significant challenges including cost concerns (evidenced by Uber's migration away from ClickHouse to Apache Pinot), limited materialized view functionality for complex joins, and the looming threat from PostgreSQL's OLAP extensions like pg_duckdb. He concludes that while ClickHouse is currently a robust solution, its long-term position may be squeezed between PostgreSQL for smaller deployments and Pinot/Druid for enterprise scale.
key_points: ClickHouse's popularity stems from three factors: raw speed, single-command installation experience, and operational simplicity compared to JVM-based competitors like Druid and Pinot
The system occupies a unique middle ground between embedded OLAP (DuckDB) and distributed OLAP (Pinot/Druid) as a single-process realtime OLAP system
Uber is migrating away from ClickHouse to Apache Pinot due to cost and performance challenges at scale, despite initial 50% savings over ELK
ClickHouse's materialized views only trigger updates when the main table in a join is written to, limiting sophisticated use cases
PostgreSQL's emerging OLAP extensions (pg_duckdb, pg_lakehouse) pose the biggest long-term threat by targeting ClickHouse's sweet spot of single-node scale-up with great developer experience
money_quotes: They have the mentality of a team on a budget. Like they didn't have 1000s of machines to throw at the problem. They had to make it work on what they had.
They've occupied a really nice spot between embedded OLAP systems like DuckDB and distributed realtime OLAP systems like Apache Pinot and Apache Druid.
It's surprising to me that there aren't more single-process realtime OLAP systems out there; it seems obvious in hindsight.
A great DX is nice and all, but does it scale and is it easy to operate?
The operational flexibility to scale up or out without adding a bunch of services is really valuable.
Stories like these are somewhat alarming for large-scale enterprises.
If PostgreSQL takes ClickHouse's single-node and small-scale usage, and systems like Pinot and Druid take its large-scale market, there's not much left.
posts.key_insight: ClickHouse's buzz comes from exceptional developer experience and operational simplicity, but its long-term viability is threatened by PostgreSQL's OLAP extensions from below and enterprise-scale systems like Pinot from above.

posts.summary: This interview features Philippe Noël, CEO of ParadeDB, discussing how they're building Elasticsearch alternatives as PostgreSQL extensions. ParadeDB offers pg_search for BM25 full-text search and pg_analytics/pg_lakehouse for columnar analytics, allowing users to avoid ETL pipelines to separate search infrastructure. Noël explains that PostgreSQL's extensibility allows it to stay current with data innovations while maintaining core stability. He discusses the recently announced pg_duckdb project, expressing cautious optimism but concern that MotherDuck may be too DuckDB-centric. The company is currently focused on commercial licenses and self-hosted deployments, with plans for BYOC solutions and Postgres platform partnerships.
key_points: ParadeDB builds Elasticsearch functionality (full-text search, analytics) as PostgreSQL extensions, eliminating the need for separate ETL pipelines
PostgreSQL's extension ecosystem enables innovation without compromising core stability - extensions can move fast while core maintains quality
Search and analytics use cases are fundamentally similar from the customer perspective: enabling users to filter data for insights or actions
The pg_duckdb project is promising but Noël worries MotherDuck may not have sufficient Postgres expertise to build it correctly
Building analytics properly in Postgres is extremely difficult - harder than what ParadeDB or most companies can currently achieve
money_quotes: In my view Postgres isn't having a renaissance but is rather 'coming of age'. The last 30 years of smart design decisions have slowly made it the best open-source RDBMS.
Extensions enable Postgres to stay current with innovations in the data space while also maintaining extreme care to keep core as small and robust as possible.
We believe customers should pick a product by thinking about their business need(s) rather than about the infrastructure of the product itself.
Building analytics in Postgres is really hard. Doing it properly is much harder than what ParadeDB could do today.
I am worried that MotherDuck, who seems to be the primary company driving this, does not care enough about Postgres to really make the project what it needs to be.
Hacking on Postgres is really hard. It's hard to find developers who know Postgres core.
The clear separation of work granted by extensions allow Postgres to uniquely accomplish the best of both worlds: Stay relevant while being rock-solid.
posts.key_insight: PostgreSQL's extension architecture enables the database to innovate rapidly in areas like search and analytics while preserving the stability and reliability that made it dominant.

posts.summary: Riccomini argues that modular monoliths offer a viable alternative to the reflexive jump to microservices that many growing companies make. Drawing on painful personal experiences at LinkedIn and WePay, he contends that microservices often just trade one set of problems for another—tight coupling, complex tooling, and operational overhead. The key insight is that monoliths become spaghetti not because modularity is impossible, but because we lack the tooling to enforce it. He outlines what modular monolith tooling would need: incremental builds, code isolation enforcement, and database boundary management. Rather than an either-or choice, modular monoliths can serve as a stepping stone that extends a monolith's useful life or provides a cleaner path to services.
key_points: Microservices often recreate the same coupling and complexity problems they were meant to solve, just in a distributed form
The real reason monoliths become unmaintainable is lack of tooling, not an inherent architectural flaw
Modular monoliths require specific tooling: incremental builds/tests, code isolation enforcement, and database boundary management
Database isolation is often the hardest part of any monolith decomposition and lacks good tooling today
A well-maintained monolith with 7±2 services is often preferable to thousands of microservices
money_quotes: You have a fever, and the only prescription is more microservices.
I have microservice fatigue.
Yes, writing modular code is a good idea, and yes it's obvious. Yet, we're not doing it.
Rather than fixing the monolithic code, we've jumped straight to microservices. Why? I think the answer is tooling.
In practice microservices can be just as tough to wrangle as monoliths.
If a developer breaks the monolith, they've broken it for everyone.
We've spent decades building tooling for microservices. It's time to give monoliths the same respect.
posts.key_insight: Monoliths don't fail because modularity is impossible—they fail because we've invested in microservice tooling instead of building the tools that would make modular monoliths work.

posts.summary: This interview with JP Kadarkarai, creator of FizzBee, explores the challenges of formal verification in distributed systems and why existing tools like TLA+ have failed to gain mainstream adoption. JP describes how his experience at Google with large-scale systems like GFS, Bigtable, and Spanner revealed the importance of formal verification, while his later work at Lyft and Clumio showed how the industry often adopts distributed architectures without theoretical foundations. FizzBee aims to make formal methods practical by using Python-like syntax instead of TLA+'s mathematical notation, automatically generating visualizations, and enabling model-based testing. The conversation also touches on how AI-assisted coding will increase the need for formal verification tools.
key_points: TLA+ adoption is hindered by its mathematical syntax and semantics that feel alien to most programmers, making it difficult to get team buy-in for spec reviews
FizzBee uses Python-like syntax to lower the barrier to entry, with the goal of making formal verification faster than writing traditional design documents
The microservices and NoSQL revolution has led many companies to adopt distributed architectures without solid theoretical foundations, causing consistency issues
FizzBee can automatically generate comprehensive tests from validated models, delivering TDD benefits without extra effort
As AI-assisted coding becomes prevalent, formal verification tools like FizzBee will be essential for validating that generated code behaves as intended
money_quotes: When there's a tradeoff between quality and time to market, time to market almost always takes precedence.
After leaving Google, I noticed a gap in the broader industry, where microservices and NoSQL databases were often adopted without a solid theoretical foundation.

The main reason was the complexity of TLA+'s syntax and semantics. Its syntax isn't intuitive—it relies heavily on ASCII representations of mathematical symbols, which can feel alien to most programmers.
The time taken to model a system in FizzBee is often less than the time it takes to write a traditional design document.
Fixing them with code changes like retries or locks didn't address the root cause—it often just moved the problem elsewhere.
As AI-assisted coding becomes more prevalent, validating that the code performs as intended will be essential for AI adoption.
posts.key_insight: Formal verification will only achieve mainstream adoption when tools prioritize developer ergonomics over mathematical rigor, meeting engineers where they already are.

posts.summary: Riccomini analyzes Confluent's WarpStream acquisition through the lens of a broader industry shift toward flexible deployment options. He argues that modern infrastructure must support multiple deployment models—embedded, single-node, clustered, SaaS, BYOC, and self-hosted—rather than forcing customers into a single approach. Using ClickHouse as the exemplar, he shows how one system can scale from an in-process library to distributed clusters while supporting all deployment modes. The post identifies technical enablers like zero-disk architecture, Rust/C++ adoption, composable data systems, and WebAssembly that make this flexibility achievable. His conclusion is stark: infrastructure vendors without flexible deployment options will fail.
key_points: Confluent's WarpStream acquisition makes strategic sense because Kora (their SaaS engine) cannot easily support BYOC deployment, while WarpStream can
The ideal approach is building a single system that accommodates flexible deployment rather than maintaining multiple codebases like Confluent now must
ClickHouse exemplifies the future: it runs embedded, single-node, clustered, self-hosted, BYOC, and as SaaS—all from one codebase
The flexible deployment trend is spreading across the industry: pglite for PostgreSQL, Neon's object storage tier, MotherDuck scaling DuckDB to the cloud
Technical advances enabling this shift include zero-disk architecture, Rust/C++ adoption, composable data systems, and WebAssembly
money_quotes: Acquisition motivation and communication are rarely the same.
There's no one-size-fits-all deployment offering.
While there's no one-size-fits-all deployment offering, I suspect we can still build a single system that can accommodate flexible deployment.
I really didn't understand why everyone was so excited about ClickHouse until I dug into it. Then it clicked: you can run ClickHouse as a single node.
In hindsight, it's a comically obvious gap in the realtime online analytical processing (OLAP) space.
It scales from a single in-process library all the way to a distributed cluster. This is the future.

Infrastructure vendors need to ask themselves: does our system deploy self-managed, BYOC, and as a SaaS cloud? Can we support in-process embedded, single-node, and clustered execution? If not, you're cooked.
posts.key_insight: Modern infrastructure must be built from the ground up to support the full spectrum of deployment options—from embedded libraries to distributed clusters to multi-tenant SaaS—or risk obsolescence.

posts.summary: Riccomini reflects on the evolution of data pipelines, noting that simpler batch ETL approaches are making a comeback for smaller organizations. He references his 2019 QCon talk outlining stages of pipeline maturity, emphasizing that many companies don't need to progress beyond basic batch processing. The post highlights how pipeline ownership has shifted from dedicated data engineering teams to ML, AI, and product engineers who prefer Python-centric tools. He introduces dlt (data load tool) as a promising solution that fits modern workflows while incorporating SDLC best practices. While acknowledging challenges like duplication and unclear ownership, Riccomini sees this democratization of data pipelines as a positive trend driven by data consumers wanting more control.
key_points: Most organizations progress through predictable stages of data pipeline maturity, but many don't need to advance beyond basic batch processing
WePay successfully ran a 15-minute interval batch pipeline on Airflow for years, proving sophisticated tooling isn't always necessary
Pipeline ownership has shifted from dedicated data engineering teams to ML, AI, analytics, and product engineers
Tools like dlt aim to bring the Python library revolution that transformed ML/AI to the data engineering space
Decentralized pipeline ownership creates challenges around duplication, inefficiency, and unclear ownership as employees come and go
money_quotes: For many smaller organizations, these tools are overkill.
Developers are thinking: if I can get by with a small query engine (DuckDB), maybe I can get by with a small ETL tool.
These days, we're asking ML, AI, analytics, and product engineers to build data pipelines.
It ran at 15m intervals and it was remarkably effective.
Data consumers want control of their data pipelines.
If everyone is managing their own data pipelines, there will be duplication.
The pipelines could become a tangled mess with no clear ownership as employees come and go.
posts.key_insight: The democratization of data pipelines through lightweight, Python-native tools like dlt reflects a broader shift where data consumers increasingly want direct control over their data flows, even if it means accepting some organizational messiness.

posts.summary: Chris Riccomini argues that virtual machines, containers, and serverless technologies are undergoing significant improvements that will reshape cloud computing. He highlights three key areas of advancement: GPU checkpointing (notably NVIDIA's cuda-checkpoint), unikernels enabling faster cold starts, and VM migration tools like Loophole Labs' Architect. These developments address longstanding pain points around slow cold starts, incomplete state snapshots, and poor GPU support. Riccomini predicts these improvements will drive multi-cloud adoption and potentially allow serverless functions to supplant traditional service-oriented architectures.
key_points: Current VMs, containers, and serverless models 'kind of work' for many use cases but truly work for few due to limitations in cold start times, snapshots, and GPU support
NVIDIA's open-source cuda-checkpoint tool solves the difficult problem of capturing GPU memory state during snapshots
Unikernels like Unikraft now offer single-digit millisecond boot times and fast snapshotting, dramatically improving serverless cold starts
Loophole Labs' Architect can migrate VM state faster than spot instance preemption occurs, enabling significant cost savings
These improvements will drive multi-cloud adoption as state becomes easier to migrate and GPU availability varies across providers
money_quotes: Until recently, these technologies have offered fairly generic features. Consequently, they kind of work for many use cases, but truly work for relatively few.
Moving your workload to a different machine (or cloud) could save money, unlock better GPUs, or speed up training and inference.
As developers execute operations on a GPU, the GPU's memory accumulates state. This GPU data is very difficult to read directly, which poses a problem if you wish to snapshot a machine's state.
Architect purports to migrate faster than a spot instance preemption occurs, which would allow for huge cost savings for many workloads.
We will, for example, see more multi-cloud adoption as state is easier to migrate and GPUs are harder to come by.
Serverless functions might supplant service oriented applications, too.
posts.key_insight: Recent advances in GPU checkpointing, unikernels, and VM migration are finally addressing the specific requirements of modern workloads, setting the stage for more flexible multi-cloud deployments and broader serverless adoption.

posts.summary: Riccomini argues that despite DuckDB's popularity among data engineers for its portability, speed, and ability to query remote storage, it fundamentally cannot serve as an enterprise data warehouse. The deployment model of installing DuckDB on individual laptops and granting bucket access is untenable for organizations where everyone from PMs to finance needs data access. While most queries are small, the most valuable enterprise queries—financial reconciliation, recommendations, advertising—require scale DuckDB cannot provide. MotherDuck is building the centralized deployment and scaling layer DuckDB needs, but this essentially makes them another cloud data warehouse competing with giants like Snowflake and Databricks. DuckDB excels as middleware, similar to SQLite, but enterprises should view it as a tool rather than a warehouse replacement.
key_points: DuckDB's deployment model doesn't work for enterprises—you can't install it on every laptop and grant everyone bucket access
While most queries are small, the most valuable queries in an organization are large and drive revenue, which DuckDB can't handle
MotherDuck is building centralized deployment and scaling, making them effectively another cloud warehouse that happens to use DuckDB
The switching cost from existing data warehouses is high; it's often cheaper to optimize your current warehouse than migrate
DuckDB is excellent middleware (like SQLite for columnar data) but shouldn't be mistaken for a data warehouse product
money_quotes: DuckDB is essentially SQLite for columnar data.
It's untenable to install DuckDB on everyone's laptop, grant everyone access to data lake buckets, and ask them to run queries from the CLI.
What they don't say is that the most valuable queries in an organization are large: financial reconciliation, recommendation systems, advertising, and others. These are the revenue drivers.
They might comprise a minority of all the queries an organization runs, but they make the money.
As much as MotherDuck would like to be the DuckDB vendor, they're a cloud data warehouse that just happens to use DuckDB.
It's often easier to cut costs in your existing data warehouse by auditing queries and data retention.
MotherDuck has picked a fight with some of the nastiest apex predators out there: Snowflake, BigQuery, and Databricks.
It's amazing middleware, much like SQLite. I don't see it as a data warehouse, though.
posts.key_insight: DuckDB is excellent middleware for columnar analytics, but its deployment model and scalability limitations mean it cannot replace enterprise data warehouses—the most valuable queries require scale that only centralized cloud solutions can provide.

posts.summary: Riccomini argues that analytics engineers and data engineers should be merged back into a single role, reversing the split that occurred over the past decade. He contends that the analytics engineer role, focused primarily on transformations, doesn't provide enough value to justify dedicated headcount. Several factors drove the original bifurcation: the shift to ELT architectures, Kafka adoption, shift-left philosophy, and vendors creating roles to sell products. Now that ZIRP has ended, data pipelines are commoditized, and LLMs can answer many data questions, the conditions that justified separate roles have changed. Tools like dltHub and tabsdata are emerging to facilitate this reunification.
key_points: The analytics engineer role is too limited in scope and too far removed from revenue generation to justify full headcount
The ETL-to-ELT shift and data lake adoption enabled data engineers to ignore transformations while giving analytics engineers something to do
ZIRP ending forced CFOs to scrutinize the cost of analyst and data teams
Data pipeline work is now commoditized with many vendors available to handle it
LLMs and data chatbots are increasingly replacing analysts for answering data questions
money_quotes: I've long held that creating the 'analytics engineer' role was a mistake.
I don't believe that this set of activities is enough value to justify a full headcount; it's too limited in scope and too far removed from revenue generation.
Dumping garbage into an object store made it easy for data engineers to ignore transformations and gave analytics engineers something to do.
dbtLabs, Motherduck, and other MDS vendors were all too willing to create a new role to sell their products, which dovetailed nicely with analyst's desire to be engineers and get paid more.
LLMs are replacing analysts in some cases. Screech all you want, but it's happening.
It's time to merge data engineers and analytics engineers back into a single data team that's responsible for E, T, and L.
I expect to see a collapse of data engineering and analytics engineering back to a single team in the next few years.
posts.key_insight: The economic and technological conditions that justified splitting data warehouse teams into data engineers and analytics engineers have reversed, making reunification both inevitable and necessary.

posts.summary: The post explores the long-standing challenge of creating a distributed filesystem with POSIX semantics, which would simplify building distributed systems by allowing them to treat remote storage like local files. While object storage like S3 provides the necessary scale and durability, it lacks POSIX compatibility, forcing developers to either integrate S3 directly into their systems or use imperfect FUSE/NFS wrappers. The growth of Kubernetes and AI workloads has intensified demand for such a solution. Riccomini highlights Regatta Storage as a promising contender that could bridge this gap with better POSIX support and built-in caching. He expects increased competition in this space, with enough market opportunity for multiple winners across different use cases.
key_points: A distributed POSIX-compatible filesystem would allow stateless services to offload all storage, dramatically simplifying distributed system design
Current approaches either require rewriting storage layers for S3 integration or use FUSE/NFS wrappers that implement only partial POSIX semantics
Kubernetes adoption and AI workloads with multi-modal data have significantly increased demand for scalable, POSIX-compatible distributed filesystems
Regatta Storage represents a promising new approach with NFS-based access, generic caching, and files stored as normal objects rather than opaque blocks
The market is large enough to support multiple winners serving different use cases despite similar underlying technology
money_quotes: You know, we wouldn't need any of this if we had a distributed filesystem that worked.
A scalable remote filesystem with normal POSIX semantics would let us build distributed systems that were stateless services; we could use the distributed filesystem to store everything.
Object stores are still missing POSIX semantics, though. You can't take any old system that uses filesystem I/O libraries and use S3 as its storage layer.

Direct object storage integration makes sense for systems like databases, whose primary job is to store and query data. But for systems, frameworks, and libraries that just need to read and write files as part of a broader workload, rewriting the storage layer for object storage is too burdensome.
Kubernetes has made every system distributed.
I think we're finally getting to the point where we have both the technology and the demand to get us what we need: a distributed POSIX-compatible filesystem.
posts.key_insight: The convergence of mature object storage technology and growing demand from Kubernetes and AI workloads is finally making distributed POSIX-compatible filesystems achievable, even if complete compatibility remains elusive.

posts.summary: Chris Riccomini argues that S3-based data lakes are becoming the modern equivalent of SFTP for sharing data with external customers. Drawing from his experience at WePay, he describes how customer data sharing requests are ubiquitous but historically under-invested in by SaaS companies. He highlights emerging startups like Prequel that focus on exporting customer data to S3 in Parquet format, enabling customers to query their data using modern lakehouse tools. Riccomini suggests this pattern could drive broader lakehouse adoption and give analytics engineers revenue-generating work.
key_points: Customer data sharing is a common but neglected need across SaaS companies, with customers wanting their data in various formats from APIs to CSV dumps

Modern data lakehouses built on S3, Iceberg, and Parquet provide SFTP-like benefits (fast transfers, atomic uploads, central access control) with added advantages of strong typing and native query engine support
Startups like Prequel are emerging to solve the 'data export platform' problem that SaaS vendors have historically rolled their own solutions for
Customer data lakes could be a driving force for lakehouse adoption as companies initially adopt these tools to access SaaS data, then expand to their own data
Schema evolution and data contracts remain challenges, but companies like Gable.ai and Vakamo are working on solutions
money_quotes: I hit the roof: our data pipeline (and team) wasn't built for this.
Despite all this demand, 'reporting', as we called it, was rarely invested in.
Customers want data from their SaaS vendors. Yet, for the past decade (longer, really), SaaS vendors have been left to roll their own solutions.

Offering customers data in a data lake format maintains some of the benefits that SFTP has historically provided: data transfers are very fast, access can be managed centrally in the catalog, and files can be atomically exposed to customers.
Customer data lakes might actually end up being a driving force for lakehouse adoption.
Giving a customer's analysts DuckDB and pointing them at an S3 bucket is pretty compelling.
These lakehouses could also give analytics engineers something to do—something that drives revenue.
posts.key_insight: S3-based data lakehouses are emerging as the modern, standardized way for SaaS companies to share data with customers, potentially driving broader lakehouse adoption while finally addressing a long-neglected but universal customer need.

posts.summary: Riccomini argues that legacy infrastructure vendors like Confluent are structurally unable to compete with lean, cloud-native startups like WarpStream. Using Confluent's acquisition of WarpStream as a case study, he explains how organizational baggage, the innovator's dilemma, and Conway's law prevent incumbents from building simpler, cheaper solutions that would cannibalize their existing business. The post contends that cloud-native architectures combined with AI-enabled engineering productivity have created a new class of 'apex predator' startups that can build competitive products with a fraction of the headcount. Riccomini warns that acquiring these nimble competitors may only delay the inevitable commoditization of infrastructure.
key_points: WarpStream built a Kafka-compatible BYOC solution with a handful of engineers in 18 months that could serve over 50% of Kafka use cases—something Confluent's organizational structure prevented them from building
Confluent's attempt to compete (Freight) was constrained to their cloud because they built on legacy tech (Kora), while WarpStream could offer true BYOC support
The innovator's dilemma traps incumbents: high-margin enterprise customers demand exotic features, preventing pivots to simpler, cheaper solutions that would serve the majority
Cloud-native efficiencies plus AI-enabled developers create a multiplier effect that makes lean startups dramatically more competitive against legacy companies with thousands of employees
Kafka itself is being commoditized by multiple competitors (Bufstream, AutoMQ, Redpanda, S2), and as these companies move up the platform stack, Confluent will face even more efficient competition
money_quotes: I believe Confluent didn't build WarpStream because it wasn't set up, as an organization, to build such a product.

WarpStream was in the process of building a cheaper (and arguably better) version of Confluent's platform with a handful of engineers that could service the majority of Confluent customers' use cases. That's a pretty staggering statement.

Why would Confluent build Freight without BYOC support? I think the answer is that it was too encumbered by its legacy tech stack, its customers (the innovator's dilemma), and its organization (Conway's law).

If Confluent could service the majority of their users with a product that looked like WarpStream, what happens to the Kora team, the legacy Kafka teams, the entire sales motion, the marketing, and so on? Such a pivot would be seismic.
It's simply much easier to build very lean, very efficient companies now.
Legacy companies built their teams before the AI shift happened. It's harder and more painful to turn the ship.
Kafka, itself, is commoditized.
Legacy infrastructure companies are going to have a tough time competing with these new cloud native, AI-enabled, post-ZIRP companies.
posts.key_insight: Legacy infrastructure vendors are structurally trapped by their own success—their organizations, tech stacks, and enterprise customers prevent them from building the simpler, cheaper cloud-native solutions that lean AI-enabled startups can ship in months with a fraction of the headcount.

posts.summary: This interview with Epsio CEO Gilad Kleinman explores incremental view maintenance (IVM) and streaming SQL technologies. Kleinman explains how streaming SQL differs from traditional batch processing by incrementally updating query results rather than reprocessing entire datasets. Epsio's approach is distinctive in that it connects to existing PostgreSQL/MySQL databases via CDC, processes changes externally, and writes materialized results back to the source database. The conversation covers how IVM fits into the broader landscape of streaming databases versus stream processors, the technical approaches behind differential dataflow and DBSP, and why IVM is expanding beyond traditional streaming use cases into customer-facing analytics and real-time reporting.
key_points: Streaming SQL processes changes incrementally rather than reprocessing entire datasets, enabling orders of magnitude faster and cheaper query results for predefined queries

Epsio differentiates by augmenting existing PostgreSQL/MySQL deployments rather than requiring migration to a new streaming database, consuming CDC streams and writing results back to the original database
The consolidation mechanism in streaming SQL prevents overwhelming source databases by batching multiple changes into single updates rather than writing each change individually

IVM use cases are shifting from traditional streaming scenarios like fraud detection toward customer-facing analytics, data transformations, and real-time reporting where query performance and cost savings are the primary drivers
Both differential dataflow and DBSP enable highly parallel processing with strong consistency guarantees and support for iterative/recursive queries that older stream processors lack
money_quotes: No matter how fast or efficient a traditional database is, if you are running a heavy query and most of the dataset hasn't changed since the last run, there is a lot of wasted compute.

By sitting 'behind' the existing database, reading CDC logs, and writing back results to the original database, users can integrate Epsio without worrying about affecting anything other than the results tables it needs to maintain.

We've seen companies make customer-facing analytics 100x faster, drop infrastructure costs by an order of magnitude, and turn reports that took hours to execute into ones that complete in under a second.

Adopting new database technologies is a pretty scary endeavor. We found that asking companies to install a new extension (that could potentially crash) on their production database was a pretty big ask to make.

I believe IVMs offer a pretty fundamental change in how organizations can work with their data—building data structures around specific queries vs. trying to store data to be 'generically' fast for any query.
Doing streaming SQL at scale for real-world use cases is no easy task.
posts.key_insight: Incremental view maintenance is evolving from niche streaming use cases to become a fundamental approach for delivering fast, cost-effective analytics by building query-specific data structures rather than optimizing for generic query patterns.

posts.summary: Riccomini argues that the data ecosystem has fractured into too many specialized catalogs—schema registries, service catalogs, information_schema, and traditional data catalogs—that all fundamentally manage metadata about data. Drawing from his experience building Recap, he observes these systems are 80% the same despite serving different use cases. He points to emerging convergence as evidence: Backstage expanding beyond microservices, Datahub adopting Iceberg's REST API, and Confluent's Tableflow speaking Iceberg. While hopeful that Iceberg's REST API could become the lingua franca, he pragmatically predicts we'll end up with catalog gateways instead of true unification.
key_points: Schema registries, service catalogs, information_schema, and data discovery tools all fundamentally manage metadata about data—they're 80% the same despite different names
Each catalog type has distinct requirements: registries need low latency and schema compatibility tracking, OLTP metadata needs speed, OLAP metadata prioritizes lineage and access controls
Convergence is already happening: Backstage expanded beyond microservices, Datahub is adopting Iceberg REST API, LakeKeeper added data contracts
After 10-15 years of data integration, pipelines are now supply chains flowing through OLTP, streaming, batch, and warehouse systems—separate catalogs no longer make sense
Iceberg's REST API might become the unifying standard, but catalog gateways are the more likely outcome
money_quotes: I began to realize that these systems were all data catalogs. Some called themselves registries, others were information_schema, and many called themselves catalogs. But they were all handling metadata about your data; they seemed 80% the same.
I'm not convinced that each of these use cases deserves its own distinct catalog. Each use case has separate requirements, but it seems to me that one system could service them all.
We spent the last 10-15 years doing data integration. Data pipelines are supply chains now.
All of our data (and metadata) flows through OLTP, streaming systems, batch, and data warehouse systems. It no longer makes sense to have a separate catalog for each use case.
The optimist in me hopes the convergence continues; 'data catalog' should mean only one thing.

If Iceberg's REST API evolves fast enough, it might become the lingua franca that we need. The realist in me knows that this is unlikely. Instead, we'll probably end up with catalog gateways or proxies.
posts.key_insight: The proliferation of specialized data catalogs—registries, service catalogs, information schemas, and discovery tools—is an artificial fragmentation of what should be a unified metadata layer, and market forces are already driving convergence around standards like Iceberg's REST API.

posts.summary: Chris Riccomini argues that AI IDEs like Windsurf and Cursor face a commoditization threat due to near-zero switching costs from their VS Code foundation. However, he believes differentiation is possible through specialized partnerships and proprietary training data. He identifies a 'virtuous cycle' problem where LLMs perform poorly on new or proprietary software, inadvertently reinforcing adoption of legacy tools. The solution lies in IDE companies partnering with software vendors to provide better LLM support for emerging technologies, and extending this approach to enterprise internal codebases.
key_points: Both Windsurf and Cursor are VS Code forks with nearly identical interfaces, making switching costs almost zero
LLMs struggle with new infrastructure and proprietary software due to insufficient public training data, creating a virtuous cycle that favors legacy tools
AI IDEs could build moats by partnering with vendors for specialized integrations, fine-tuned models, and custom tooling
Enterprise codebases represent another opportunity since internal code isn't publicly available for LLM training
GitHub has competitive advantages with access to private repositories but has struggled to execute effectively with Copilot
money_quotes: Since both Windsurf and Cursor are forks of VS Code with nearly identical interfaces, the switching cost is nearly zero.
It's easy to think these IDEs are destined to be commodities. I don't think so.
LLMs really struggle with dataframe libraries that aren't Pandas; they hallucinate and assume you're using Pandas.
This dynamic is interesting: LLMs struggle with new infrastructure and tools.
A poor LLM experience with new and proprietary software makes for an interesting virtuous cycle.
A better experience increases the adoption cost for new software (or decreases the cost of using legacy software).
A partnership between Windsurf and a company bringing a new software product to market would be symbiotic.
Copilot has lagged behind its competitors despite strong competitive advantages.
posts.key_insight: AI IDEs can escape commoditization by partnering with software vendors to provide superior LLM experiences for new and proprietary tools, breaking the virtuous cycle that currently favors legacy software.

posts.summary: Incremental view maintenance (IVM) is gaining significant momentum with new implementations from Materialize, Feldera, Epsio, and others, as well as PostgreSQL's experimental pg_ivm extension. Traditional approaches to view updates—either recomputing on every read or periodically refreshing entire datasets—are wasteful and slow. IVM solves this by processing only the deltas, potentially making updates millions of times faster. The post traces the evolution from bag algebra approaches to modern frameworks: timely dataflow provides flexible time modeling, differential dataflow adds version tracking for selective computation reuse, and DBSP offers a simpler circuit-based model that can convert any batch SQL to incremental queries. While these technologies underpin many modern IVM systems, adoption challenges remain due to complexity.
key_points: IVM processes only changed data (deltas) rather than recomputing entire datasets, potentially achieving speedups of 10 million times for large databases with small changes
Traditional bag algebra approaches work for simple queries but struggle with complex, recursive, or nested structures
Timely dataflow's key innovation is separating data and control planes, allowing concurrent processing at different points in time
Differential dataflow tracks multiple versions of data states in a partial order, enabling selective reuse of prior computations
DBSP uses digital signal processing concepts to prove that any batch SQL can be automatically converted to incremental queries using just four operators
money_quotes: In practice this may be an improvement of several orders of magnitude. For our example above |DB|≅10⁹ and |ΔDB|≅10², this can make QΔ 10 million times faster!
Cheap updates allow us to refresh materialized views more frequently. In doing so, we can keep materialized views more up to date with their source data, thereby reducing data latency.
Complex incremental queries that use joins, window functions, or recursive algorithms are very difficult to write by hand. A more systematic approach is needed.
I like to think of timely dataflow a lot like Hadoop's Map/Reduce: a powerful, but low-level framework for data processing.
DBSP can convert any batch-based SQL to an incremental query.

The progression from timely dataflow to differential dataflow and then to DBSP shows a clear trajectory: moving from highly flexible, low-level frameworks toward more structured, to easier-to-use incremental computation systems.
Differential dataflow is just really complicated.
Getting this technology into everyone's hands will be a very big deal.
posts.key_insight: Modern IVM engines built on timely dataflow, differential dataflow, and DBSP can automatically convert any batch SQL query into an efficient incremental computation, but the technology's complexity remains a barrier to widespread adoption.

posts.summary: Chris Riccomini argues that while batch processing has seen dramatic innovation over the past decade, the streaming ecosystem around Kafka has largely stagnated. Despite Kafka and Flink being over 10 years old, they remain the dominant solutions, with most recent progress focused on adoption rather than innovation. The entrenchment of Kafka's protocol as a de facto standard has created network effects that discourage fundamental rethinking. Riccomini sees S2 as a promising challenger because it's the only modern streaming solution not constrained by Kafka's protocol, and suggests technologies like Feldera and differential dataflow libraries could spark the next wave of innovation in streaming, similar to what cloud warehouses and Spark did for batch processing.
key_points: Batch processing has transformed dramatically with cloud warehouses, lakehouses, Spark, DuckDB, and composable systems, while streaming remains dominated by 10+ year old technologies
Kafka's protocol becoming a de facto standard benefits enterprises but creates barriers to fundamental innovation since concepts like partitions and leadership are baked in
The streaming industry shows signs of stagnation: same people, same companies, same technology at conferences, with predictions that 80% of expo vendors will be dead in two years
S2 is uniquely positioned as the only modern streaming solution not using Kafka's protocol, allowing them to rethink fundamentals like timestamps and per-user streams
Feldera and differential dataflow libraries like D2 represent promising computation layer improvements that could become the Spark or Snowflake of streaming
money_quotes: The pace of change in the batch processing ecosystem stands in stark contrast to its streaming counterpart.
Much of the story of the past 10 years has been about adoption rather than innovation.
It's still very difficult to write and deploy stream processing jobs; it honestly feels like it's gotten worse, not better.
One person I spoke with said that '80% of the companies in the Expo hall will be dead in two years'.
It's the same people, same companies, and same technology. There are no new ideas and no new users.

A de facto standard protocol is great for enterprises; they don't need to migrate and vendors are all forced to compete on a commoditized storage layer. But what happens when the protocol needs to change?
Eleven years later, it feels like we might be at a similar turning point with Kafka; it's widely adopted, but dated.
The next decade of stream processing could look like the last decade of batch processing: one of growth and innovation as we transition out of legacy on-prem architectures.
posts.key_insight: Kafka's protocol becoming a de facto standard has created adoption success but also innovation stagnation, and the streaming ecosystem needs unconstrained newcomers willing to rethink fundamentals to spark the next wave of progress.

posts.summary: Chris Riccomini provides an update on his various projects after a summer hiatus from newsletter writing. He has been working on the second edition of Designing Data-Intensive Applications, specifically rewriting the batch chapter and updating the streaming chapter. SlateDB development continues with new features like serializable snapshot isolation and transactions, plus Python and Go bindings. The most exciting SlateDB news is ZeroFS, a POSIX-compliant network filesystem built on top of SlateDB. His venture fund Materialized View Capital is 75% deployed and will be fully deployed in 18 months.
key_points: The batch chapter of DDIA second edition required a full rewrite while streaming updates will be more minimal, reflecting the stagnant streaming ecosystem
SlateDB has added serializable snapshot isolation, transactions support, Python and Go bindings, and a deterministic simulation tester
ZeroFS is a new POSIX-compliant network filesystem built on SlateDB that will enable zero-copy filesystem forking
Materialized View Capital is 75% deployed and will be fully deployed ~18 months from inception, ahead of the typical 3-year target
The author uses coding agents constantly and plans to write more about AI, MCP, and enterprise agent adoption
money_quotes: The updates to the streaming chapter reflect this; they'll be more minimal.
ZeroFS provides network filesystem (NFS), network block device (NBD), and Plan 9 Filesystem Protocol (9P) implementations on top of SlateDB.
SlateDB's branching and forking features mean ZeroFS will be able to provide zero-copy filesystem forking—an important feature for AI and many other use cases.
I've been reluctant to post about the topic because I don't feel I'm an expert in the subject. (Then again, it's so new that very few are.)
I use coding agents constantly, though.
An 18 month deployment for a smaller fund like MVC is not unheard of.
posts.key_insight: The streaming ecosystem has been remarkably stagnant over the past decade, while the most exciting infrastructure innovation is happening at the storage layer with projects like SlateDB enabling new possibilities like zero-copy filesystem forking.

posts.summary: Chris Riccomini interviews Hussain Sultan, co-founder of Xorq, about the fragmented DataFrame ecosystem and how Xorq addresses it through a multi-engine approach. Sultan explains that Xorq functions as a 'compute catalog' that makes expressions portable, observable, and reusable across different engines like DuckDB, DataFusion, and Pandas. The system uses content-addressed hashing inspired by Nix to enable deterministic caching and artifact reuse across queries, workflows, and users. While currently batch-focused, Xorq's architecture supports out-of-core processing and has plans for streaming engine support. The interview covers how Xorq integrates with ML workloads through Arrow Flight and UDF/UDAF machinery, positioning it as a unified system for data engineering and machine learning pipelines.
key_points: The DataFrame ecosystem remains bifurcated between single-node libraries (Pandas, R) and bolt-on APIs for distributed engines (PySpark, Snowpark), creating a need for unified abstraction
Xorq is a 'compute catalog' that stores relational + functional + semantic plans that are portable across engines, using Ibis as the user-facing expression system
Cache keys are derived by hashing expressions and source descriptors, enabling deterministic reuse across queries, workflows, and users—similar to how Nix handles build artifacts
The system can detect shared subgraphs across teams and organizations, surfacing candidates for caching and reuse at scale
ML training and inference are unified into a single relational graph using UDF/UDAF machinery, with 'fit' lowering to aggregates that emit state and 'apply' consuming that state for predictions
money_quotes: It wasn't an infra problem—it was an abstraction problem. Data has structure. Compute didn't.
Xorq is a compute catalog: a place to put the logic itself. Expressions become portable, observable, and reusable. Multi-engine. Storage-agnostic. Versioned by default.
If you can name a computation deterministically, you can look it up later and recover its full lineage to the source.
We can compare expression nodes across different graphs—even across teams—and detect shared subgraphs. That lets us surface high-value candidates for caching and reuse across an organization.
DataFrame APIs excel for feature engineering and early-stage preprocessing, but late-stage preprocessing nearly always shifts to tensor operations.
In the multi-engine batch processing world, our early design partners report speeding up dynamic dbt-style workloads by 5-15x versus Jinja-based SQL model generation.
Our catalog-first approach makes reuse, optimization, and governance first-class, while staying minimally invasive to how teams already run on SQL engines.
posts.key_insight: The DataFrame ecosystem's fragmentation is fundamentally an abstraction problem—compute lacks the structure that data has—and content-addressed 'compute catalogs' can make expressions portable, cacheable, and reusable across engines and teams.

posts.summary: Chris Riccomini argues that the Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an unnecessary new standard when existing solutions like OpenAPI, gRPC, and CLIs already serve the same purpose of teaching LLMs to interact with software systems. He finds MCP's categorization of resources, prompts, and tools arbitrary and confusing, noting that the same flight search example appears in their documentation as both a tool and a resource. While acknowledging arguments about context window limitations and poor documentation, he's skeptical these justify a new protocol, especially as context windows expand rapidly. He concludes that developers should focus on building better tools using existing standards rather than adopting MCP.
key_points: MCP's categories (resources, prompts, tools) are arbitrary and confusing—the same functionality can be represented multiple ways
Existing standards like OpenAPI, gRPC, and CLIs already accomplish what MCP does, and LLMs are already proficient at using them
Context window limitations that justify MCP are temporary—models have grown from 1 million to 2 million tokens in a year
The argument that MCP fixes poor documentation is flawed: the same developers who wrote bad OpenAPI specs will write bad MCP specs
Benchmarks show MCP vs CLI is essentially a wash, with both achieving 100% success rates
money_quotes: This begs the question: why do we need MCP for tool definitions? We already have OpenAPI, gRPC, and CLIs.
ChatGPT is actually better at CLIs than most humans—just watch Codex fly through sed, awk, and grep commands.
Viewed from this lens, MCP seems like a temporary kludge.
The argument seems to be that the same people that wrote the bad OpenAPI specs are going to write good MCP specs. I don't buy it.
There's no law that LLMs need a new protocol to interact with software.
The protocol is just plumbing. What matters is whether your tool helps or hinders the agent's ability to complete tasks.
MCP is a victim of its own success.
posts.key_insight: MCP is a solution in search of a problem—existing standards like OpenAPI and CLIs already enable LLMs to interact with software systems, making a new protocol unnecessary overhead.
